## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model QP]**
> Differentiate between an **actuator** and a **sensor** in a mechatronic system. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Actuator:** A device that converts a control signal (electrical/pneumatic/hydraulic) into physical motion, force, or torque. *Example:* DC servo motor (1 mark).
- **Sensor:** A device that detects a physical quantity (position, force, temperature) and converts it into a measurable electrical signal. *Example:* LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer) (1 mark).
- **Key distinction:** Actuator is the *output* side; sensor is the *input* side of the mechatronic loop. Both are necessary for closed-loop control (1 mark).

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, Model QP]**
> List any **three selection criteria** for choosing a sensor for a mechatronic application.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Range** ŌĆö must cover the full expected variation of the measured variable (1 mark).
2. **Accuracy & Resolution** ŌĆö must be high enough to detect the smallest change required by the control system (1 mark).
3. **Bandwidth / Response Time** ŌĆö must be at least 5ŌĆō10 times faster than the fastest expected dynamic event in the process (1 mark).

*(Alternative valid criteria: environment compatibility, linearity, cost, output type, physical size, signal conditioning needs.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Question A ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the classification of **actuators** used in mechatronic systems, with at least two examples for each category. Discuss the criteria for selecting between electrical, hydraulic, and pneumatic actuators. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A **strain gauge** with gauge factor $GF = 2.0$ and nominal resistance $R = 120 \ \Omega$ is bonded to a steel beam ($E = 200$ GPa) subjected to a stress $\sigma = 80$ MPa. It is connected in a **quarter Wheatstone bridge** with excitation voltage $V_{ex} = 10$ V. Calculate: (i) the strain, (ii) the change in resistance, and (iii) the bridge output voltage. Comment on whether this output is directly readable by a typical 0ŌĆō5 V ADC. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

**Classification of Actuators (3 marks):**
Actuators are classified by **energy domain** and **motion type**.

| Energy Source | Linear | Rotary |
|---|---|---|
| Electrical | Solenoid, Voice coil, Linear motor | DC motor, Stepper, Servo, BLDC |
| Hydraulic | Hydraulic cylinder | Hydraulic motor |
| Pneumatic | Pneumatic cylinder | Pneumatic motor / vane |
| Thermal / Other | Bimetal strip, SMA wire | Limited (mostly linear) |

**Selection Criteria (4 marks):**
1. **Force / Torque Requirement** ŌĆö Hydraulic > Pneumatic > Electrical (for force density). Hydraulic wins for $> 25$ kN loads.
2. **Speed / Bandwidth** ŌĆö Electrical actuators (servos) have highest bandwidth (kHz). Pneumatic is fast but difficult to control precisely. Hydraulic is slower due to fluid compressibility.
3. **Precision** ŌĆö Electrical (stepper/servo) gives sub-micron repeatability. Pneumatic has stiction and compressibility issues. Hydraulic requires servo-valves for precision.
4. **Environment** ŌĆö Electrical preferred in clean indoor labs; hydraulic/pneumatic in heavy industry. Pneumatic preferred where sparks are dangerous (mining).
5. **Cost & Maintenance** ŌĆö Electrical is cheapest to install; pneumatic is cheap to operate; hydraulic has high maintenance (leakage, filters).
6. **Power-to-Weight Ratio** ŌĆö Hydraulic is best for heavy machinery; electrical for portable/mobile.

**Model Solution for (b) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

**Given:** $GF = 2.0$, $R = 120 \ \Omega$, $E = 200$ GPa, $\sigma = 80$ MPa, $V_{ex} = 10$ V.

**Step (i) ŌĆö Strain (2 marks):**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{80 \times 10^{6}}{200 \times 10^{9}} = 4 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step (ii) ŌĆö Change in Resistance (2 marks):**

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon = 2.0 \times 4 \times 10^{-4} = 8 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\Delta R = 8 \times 10^{-4} \times 120 = 0.096 \ \Omega$$

**Step (iii) ŌĆö Output Voltage (2 marks):**

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{10}{4} \times 8 \times 10^{-4} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 2 \text{ mV}$$

**Comment on ADC Compatibility (1 mark):**
A 0ŌĆō5 V ADC has a resolution step of approximately $\frac{5 \text{ V}}{2^{10}} \approx 4.88$ mV for a 10-bit ADC. The bridge output of **2 mV is smaller than one LSB**, so the signal **cannot be directly read** ŌĆö an **instrumentation amplifier with gain $\geq 1000$** is required.

---

> **Question B ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** Define the term **mechanical structure** in the context of mechatronic design. List and briefly explain the **four major functions** that a mechanical structure must perform. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)**. List two advantages and two limitations of using an LVDT as a displacement sensor. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A **mechanical structure** in a mechatronic system is the physical framework of rigid and semi-rigid elements (frames, shafts, bearings, linkages, housings) that supports, connects, aligns, and transmits loads between sensors, actuators, and the workpiece.

**Four Major Functions (6 marks ŌĆö 1.5 each):**
1. **Load Bearing & Support** ŌĆö Holds all components in correct spatial relationship and supports the weight of the system and the external loads.
2. **Motion Transmission & Guidance** ŌĆö Provides paths for motion (linear or rotary) through bearings, guides, cams, linkages, and ball screws.
3. **Protection & Environmental Sealing** ŌĆö Shields sensitive electronics/sensors from dust, moisture, temperature, and mechanical impact via enclosures and gaskets.
4. **Heat Dissipation & Vibration Damping** ŌĆö Acts as a heat sink for motors/driver electronics and absorbs vibrations through material selection and isolation mounts.

**Model Solution for (b) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
An LVDT consists of:
- One **primary coil** $P$ wound at the center of a hollow cylindrical former.
- Two identical **secondary coils** $S_1$ and $S_2$ wound on either side of the primary.
- A movable **soft-iron core** that slides along the axis inside the former.
- The two secondaries are connected in **series-opposition** (their EMFs subtract).

**Working (2 marks):**
When the primary is energized by an AC excitation $V_{ex}$, it produces alternating flux. The flux links the two secondaries through the core. The output is

$$V_{out} = V_{S1} - V_{S2}$$

- When the core is **centered**, $V_{S1} = V_{S2}$ ŌåÆ $V_{out} = 0$.
- When the core moves **toward $S_1$**, $V_{S1} > V_{S2}$ ŌåÆ $V_{out}$ is positive.
- When the core moves **toward $S_2$**, $V_{S2} > V_{S1}$ ŌåÆ $V_{out}$ is negative.
The magnitude of $V_{out}$ is proportional to displacement, and the phase indicates direction.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Frictionless, infinite resolution, excellent linearity (┬▒0.5 %FS).
- No contact wear, high reliability, long life.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Requires AC excitation and signal demodulation (more complex than a potentiometer).
- Sensitive to external magnetic fields and temperature; the moving core adds mass.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in this Module (as flagged in KTU valuation keys):**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in formulas ŌĆö losing 1 mark per quantity (e.g., writing $F = 5 \times 1.96 = 9.8$ without units).
> 2. **Confusing gauge factor and strain** ŌĆö Gauge factor is dimensionless; the actual strain $\varepsilon = \sigma / E$ is what gets multiplied.
> 3. **Not commenting on compatibility** ŌĆö KTU requires an *engineering judgment* statement at the end of any selection/numerical problem (e.g., "1.25 safety factor ŌåÆ ACCEPTABLE").
> 4. **Omitting sketches** in long answers ŌĆö A neat block diagram or labelled sketch carries 1ŌĆō2 easy marks. Even in text answers, draw boxes with arrows.
> 5. **Mixing up efficiency formulas** ŌĆö Mechanical efficiency $\eta = P_{out} / P_{in}$; do not write $\eta = P_{in} / P_{out}$.
> 6. **Ignoring the "actuatorŌĆōsensor" pair** ŌĆö Many KTU questions specifically ask whether the *resolution* of the sensor can detect the *smallest step* of the actuator. Always compute both and compare.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- Ō£ģ Mechatronic mechanical components = **Sensors + Actuators + Mechanical Structures** (the "S-A-S" trinity).
- Ō£ģ Actuator selection depends on **Force, Speed, Precision, Environment, Cost**.
- Ō£ģ Sensor selection depends on **Range, Resolution, Accuracy, Bandwidth, Environment**.
- Ō£ģ Mechanical structure must perform **Support, Motion transmission, Protection, Damping**.
- Ō£ģ **Compatibility check** = (a) sensor resolution $\leq$ actuator smallest step, (b) actuator force $\geq$ load force ├Ś safety factor, (c) structure stiffness $\geq$ required for deflection limit.
- Ō£ģ **Hydraulic** = high force, slow, accurate with servo-valve.
- Ō£ģ **Pneumatic** = medium force, fast, low precision, low cost.
- Ō£ģ **Electrical (servo/stepper)** = best precision, cleanest operation.
- Ō£ģ LVDT: frictionless, infinite resolution, needs AC excitation.
- Ō£ģ Strain gauge: $V_{out} = (V_{ex}/4)(\Delta R/R)$ for quarter-bridge.
- Ō£ģ DC motor: $\tau = K_t I$, $V = K_e \omega$, $P = \tau \omega$.
- Ō£ģ Always state units in numerical answers and provide a final **compatibility verdict**.
- Ō£ģ A mechatronic system is only as good as its **weakest link** ŌĆö sensor, actuator, or structure.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electronic Components: Overview of Microcontrollers, Sensors, and Signal Conditioning Circuits; Interfacing Techniques and Circuit Design for Integration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT861 - MECHATRONIC SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electronic Components: Overview of Microcontrollers, Sensors, and Signal Conditioning Circuits; Interfacing Techniques and Circuit Design for Integration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Microcontrollers ŌĆö The Digital Brain of a Mechatronic System

A **microcontroller (MCU)** is a single-chip, self-contained computer that integrates a **Central Processing Unit (CPU)**, **volatile memory (RAM)**, **non-volatile program memory (ROM/Flash/EEPROM)**, **clock generation circuitry**, and a rich set of **peripheral interfaces** (GPIO, ADC, timers, UART, I┬▓C, SPI, PWM, CAN) onto one silicon die.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A microcontroller is an IC that combines the processor core, memory, and input/output peripherals on a single chip, designed for embedded real-time control of mechanical and electromechanical systems.

**Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Human Nervous System:**
Think of the microcontroller as the **brain and spinal cord** of a mechatronic system. Just like the brain receives signals from the eyes (sensors), processes them, and sends motor commands to the hand (actuators), the MCU reads sensor data, executes control algorithms (e.g., PID), and drives actuators via PWM or digital I/O.

### 1.1.1 Common 8/16/32-bit MCU Families used in Mechatronics

| Family | Typical Bit-width | Common Use in Mechatronics |
|---|---|---|
| 8051 (Intel/Atmel) | 8-bit | Legacy motor drives, simple interlocks |
| PIC (Microchip) | 8/16-bit | Low-cost sensor hubs |
| AVR (Atmel/Microchip) | 8-bit | Educational kits (Arduino) |
| ARM Cortex-M0/M3/M4 | 32-bit | Robotic controllers, BLDC drives |
| ESP32 (Tensilica Xtensa) | 32-bit dual-core | IoT-enabled mechatronics |

## 1.2 Sensors ŌĆö The Sensory Organs

A **sensor** is a transducer that converts a physical quantity (temperature, displacement, force, light, etc.) into a usable electrical signal (voltage, current, frequency, or resistance change).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** In the 2024 scheme, the distinction between **sensors** (input) and **actuators** (output) is a high-weightage concept. A sensor is always the *energy-converting element on the input side*.

**Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Human Senses:**
- A **thermistor/thermocouple** Ōēł skin sensing temperature.
- A **potentiometer / LVDT** Ōēł proprioception (knowing joint angle).
- A **strain gauge** Ōēł nerve endings in fingertips.
- A **photodiode** Ōēł the eye.
- A **MEMS accelerometer / gyroscope** Ōēł vestibular system (inner-ear balance).

## 1.3 Signal Conditioning Circuits ŌĆö The Translator

Real-world sensor outputs are typically **weak (mV-level), noisy, non-linear, and incompatible** with the input ranges of an ADC (e.g., 0ŌĆō3.3 V) or a microcontroller logic level (3.3 V / 5 V CMOS/TTL). **Signal conditioning circuits** bridge this gap.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Definition (KTU-aligned):** Signal conditioning is the manipulation of a sensor's raw output signal into a form suitable for digitization or further processing. Common operations include **amplification, filtering, isolation, linearization, level shifting, and impedance matching**.

**Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö A Translator in a Foreign Country:**
A sensor might "speak" a tiny, noisy analog dialect. The signal conditioner translates it into a clean, loud, standardized "language" (e.g., 0ŌĆō3.3 V, low impedance) that the microcontroller can directly understand.

## 1.4 Interfacing Techniques ŌĆö The Connective Tissue

**Interfacing** refers to the hardware and protocol mechanisms used to connect the microcontroller with sensors, actuators, and other MCUs. This spans **analog interfacing (ADC, PWM, comparators)**, **digital parallel interfacing (GPIO, memory-mapped buses)**, and **serial interfacing (UART, I┬▓C, SPI, CAN, USB)**.

## 1.5 Mechatronic Integration Block ŌĆö One-Page Summary

A mechatronic system is fundamentally:

$$
\text{Physical World} \xrightarrow{\text{Sensor}} \text{Raw Signal} \xrightarrow{\text{Conditioner}} \text{Clean Analog/Digital Signal} \xrightarrow{\text{MCU}} \text{Control Action} \xrightarrow{\text{Driver}} \text{Actuator} \xrightarrow{} \text{Physical World}
$$

This closed loop is the cornerstone of every robotic arm, CNC machine, ABS brake, drone, and pick-and-place system.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Microcontroller ŌĆö Internal Architecture

### 2.1.1 Harvard vs. Von Neumann Architecture

| Feature | Von Neumann | Harvard |
|---|---|---|
| Program & Data memory | Single shared bus | Separate buses |
| Speed | Bottleneck (fetch-decode overlap not possible) | Faster (concurrent fetch) |
| Example | 8086 (older) | 8051, ARM Cortex-M |
| Use in mechatronics | Rare | Universal |

Most modern MCUs (PIC, AVR, ARM Cortex-M) use a **modified Harvard** architecture.

### 2.1.2 Core Peripherals Critical to Mechatronics

1. **GPIO (General Purpose Input/Output):** Reading switches, driving LEDs, bit-banging simple protocols.
2. **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter):** Successive-Approximation Register (SAR) type is most common. Resolution: 8/10/12/16-bit.
3. **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation):** Used to drive DC motors, solenoids, LEDs. Frequency ranges from kHz to tens of kHz.
4. **Timers/Counters:** Used for event counting, debouncing, encoder reading (quadrature decoding).
5. **Communication interfaces:** UART (asynchronous, full-duplex), I┬▓C (2-wire, master-slave, Ōēż400 kHz standard, Ōēż3.4 MHz high-speed), SPI (4-wire, full-duplex, fast), CAN (robust automotive bus), USB.

### 2.1.3 MCU Selection Criteria for a Mechatronic Design

- **Clock speed** (MHz)
- **Bit-width** (8/16/32) ŌåÆ determines computation throughput
- **Memory footprint** (Flash KB, RAM KB)
- **ADC channels and resolution**
- **Number of PWM channels and timers**
- **Communication peripherals** present
- **Operating voltage** (3.3 V vs. 5 V)
- **Package and form factor**
- **Power consumption** (active/sleep current)

## 2.2 Sensors ŌĆö Classification and Working Principles

### 2.2.1 Classification

**By Output Signal Type:**
- **Analog sensors:** Output a continuous voltage/current (e.g., LM35 temperature, potentiometer).
- **Digital sensors:** Output a discrete word (e.g., DS18B20 over 1-Wire, MPU6050 over I┬▓C).

**By Power Requirement:**
- **Passive (self-generating):** Generate their own voltage (thermocouple, piezoelectric, photovoltaic cell).
- **Active (externally powered):** Need excitation (strain gauge, RTD, thermistor, Hall sensor).

**By Physical Principle:**
- Resistive (thermistor, LDR, strain gauge)
- Capacitive (capacitive proximity, MEMS accelerometer)
- Inductive (LVDT, eddy-current probe)
- Piezoelectric (force, accelerometer)
- Semiconductor (Hall-effect, photodiode, MEMS gyro)
- Optical (encoder, LIDAR, infrared)

### 2.2.2 High-Yield Sensor Equations

**Strain Gauge (piezoresistive):**

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_f \cdot \varepsilon
$$

where $G_f$ is the gauge factor (typically 2 for constantan, ~100ŌĆō200 for semiconductor gauges) and $\varepsilon$ is the mechanical strain.

**LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer) ŌĆö Displacement Sensor:**

$$
V_{\text{out}} = K_{\text{LVDT}} \cdot (V_{\text{sec1}} - V_{\text{sec2}}) = K \cdot x
$$

where $x$ is the core displacement from null and $K$ is sensitivity (V/mm).

**Thermistor (NTC) ŌĆö Steinhart-Hart Equation:**

$$
\frac{1}{T} = A + B \ln(R) + C [\ln(R)]^3
$$

where $T$ is absolute temperature in Kelvin and $A, B, C$ are constants.

**Thermocouple (Seebeck Effect):**

$$
V = \alpha_{AB} \cdot \Delta T = (\alpha_A - \alpha_B) \cdot (T_{\text{hot}} - T_{\text{cold}})
$$

where $\alpha_{AB}$ is the relative Seebeck coefficient (e.g., Type K Ōēł 41 ┬ĄV/┬░C).

**Hall-Effect Sensor:**

$$
V_H = \frac{R_H \cdot I \cdot B}{t}
$$

where $R_H$ is the Hall coefficient, $I$ is bias current, $B$ is magnetic flux density, $t$ is sensor thickness.

## 2.3 Signal Conditioning ŌĆö The Core Building Blocks

### 2.3.1 Instrumentation Amplifier (INA) ŌĆö For Strain Gauges & Bridges

Used when the sensor is in a **Wheatstone bridge** configuration and the differential signal is very small (mV) on top of a large common-mode voltage.

$$
V_{\text{out}} = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{\text{gain}}}\right) \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2} \cdot (V_1 - V_2)
$$

- $R_{\text{gain}}$ sets the gain.
- High **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)** (> 100 dB).

### 2.3.2 Active Filters ŌĆö Sallen-Key Topology

**First-Order Low-Pass (Anti-Aliasing):**

$$
f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R C}
$$

**Second-Order Sallen-Key Low-Pass:**

$$
f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}
$$

For equal-component design ($R_1 = R_2 = R$, $C_1 = C_2 = C$):

$$
f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R C}
$$

Butterworth Q = 0.7071 (maximally flat), Bessel Q = 0.5771 (linear phase), Chebyshev Q > 0.7071 (ripple).

### 2.3.3 ADC Resolution ŌĆö Quantization

For an n-bit ADC with reference voltage $V_{\text{ref}}$:

$$
\text{LSB (Resolution)} = \frac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^n}
$$

$$
V_{\text{in}} = \frac{\text{ADC code}}{2^n} \cdot V_{\text{ref}}
$$

**Quantization Noise (RMS):**

$$
V_{q,\text{rms}} = \frac{\text{LSB}}{\sqrt{12}}
$$

**Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) for a full-scale sine wave:**

$$
\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02 \cdot n + 1.76
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NyquistŌĆōShannon Sampling Theorem:** To avoid aliasing, the sampling frequency must satisfy $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{\text{max}}$. An **anti-aliasing analog filter** with cutoff below $f_s / 2$ is therefore **mandatory** before the ADC.

# SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Quantization Step and SQNR of an ADC

We start from the definition of an ideal n-bit ADC and show the relationship between resolution and effective signal-to-noise ratio.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define the full-scale input range.**

The analog input is mapped from $0$ to $V_{\text{ref}}$. The total number of discrete levels is:

$$
N_{\text{levels}} = 2^n
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Width of one Least Significant Bit (LSB).**

The voltage represented by a single LSB (quantization step) is:

$$
\Delta V = \text{LSB} = \frac{V_{\text{ref}} - 0}{2^n} = \frac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^n}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Quantization error as a sawtooth waveform.**

The error $e$ between the true analog value and the digital code lies uniformly in $[-\Delta V / 2, +\Delta V / 2]$. The probability density function is:

$$
p(e) = \frac{1}{\Delta V}, \quad e \in \left[-\frac{\Delta V}{2}, +\frac{\Delta V}{2}\right]
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Mean-square value of the quantization error.**

The mean-square error (which equals the noise variance, assuming zero-mean):

$$
\sigma_q^2 = \int_{-\Delta V / 2}^{+\Delta V / 2} e^2 \cdot p(e) \, de = \frac{1}{\Delta V} \cdot \frac{e^3}{3} \Big|_{-\Delta V / 2}^{+\Delta V / 2}
$$

Evaluating:

$$
\sigma_q^2 = \frac{1}{\Delta V} \cdot \left[\frac{(\Delta V / 2)^3}{3} - \frac{(-\Delta V / 2)^3}{3}\right] = \frac{1}{\Delta V} \cdot \frac{(\Delta V)^3}{12}
$$

$$
\sigma_q^2 = \frac{(\Delta V)^2}{12}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö RMS quantization noise voltage.**

$$
V_{q,\text{rms}} = \sigma_q = \frac{\Delta V}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^n \sqrt{12}}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Signal power of a full-scale sine wave.**

For an input $V_{\text{in}}(t) = V_p \sin(2\pi f t)$ that swings the full ADC range ($V_p = V_{\text{ref}}/2$), the RMS signal voltage is:

$$
V_{\text{sig,rms}} = \frac{V_p}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2\sqrt{2}}
$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Compute SQNR.**

$$
\text{SQNR} = \frac{V_{\text{sig,rms}}^2}{\sigma_q^2} = \frac{V_{\text{ref}}^2 / 8}{V_{\text{ref}}^2 / (12 \cdot 4^n)} = \frac{12 \cdot 4^n}{8} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 4^n
$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Convert to decibels.**

$$
\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{2} \cdot 4^n\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) + 10 n \log_{10}(4)
$$

Since $\log_{10}(4) \approx 0.60206$ and $10 \log_{10}(3/2) \approx 1.76$:

$$
\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} \approx 6.02 \, n + 1.76
$$

This is the canonical result: **each extra ADC bit adds Ōēł 6 dB of dynamic range.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Strain Gauge in a Quarter-Bridge Configuration

**Step 1 ŌĆö Strain definition.**

For a bar under axial load, mechanical strain is:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Resistance change due to strain.**

A conductor of length $L$, area $A$, and resistivity $\rho$ has resistance $R = \rho L / A$. Under strain both geometry and piezoresistivity change:

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_f \cdot \varepsilon
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Quarter-bridge output voltage.**

A single active gauge $R_g$ in one arm of a Wheatstone bridge (with the other three arms = $R$):

$$
V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{ex}} \left[ \frac{R_g}{R_g + R} - \frac{R}{R + R} \right]
$$

Substituting $R_g = R(1 + G_f \varepsilon)$:

$$
V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{ex}} \left[ \frac{1 + G_f \varepsilon}{2 + G_f \varepsilon} - \frac{1}{2} \right]
$$

$$
V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{ex}} \cdot \frac{G_f \varepsilon}{2(2 + G_f \varepsilon)}
$$

For small strains ($G_f \varepsilon \ll 1$):

$$
V_{\text{out}} \approx \frac{V_{\text{ex}} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon}{4}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Solve for strain from measured output.**

$$
\varepsilon \approx \frac{4 \cdot V_{\text{out}}}{V_{\text{ex}} \cdot G_f}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: First-Order RC Low-Pass Filter Transfer Function

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply KCL at the output node.**

For an input $V_{\text{in}}$ through resistor $R$ to capacitor $C$ to ground, with $V_{\text{out}}$ across the capacitor:

$$
\frac{V_{\text{in}} - V_{\text{out}}}{R} = C \frac{dV_{\text{out}}}{dt}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Take the Laplace transform.**

$$
\frac{V_{\text{in}}(s) - V_{\text{out}}(s)}{R} = sC \cdot V_{\text{out}}(s)
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Solve for the transfer function.**

$$
V_{\text{out}}(s) (1 + sRC) = V_{\text{in}}(s)
$$

$$
H(s) = \frac{V_{\text{out}}(s)}{V_{\text{in}}(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + sRC}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Identify the cutoff frequency.**

In the standard form $H(s) = \frac{1}{1 + s/\omega_c}$, we identify:

$$
\omega_c = \frac{1}{RC}
$$

$$
f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}
$$

The magnitude response is $|H(j\omega)| = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega/\omega_c)^2}}$, and at $\omega = \omega_c$ the gain drops by $-3$ dB.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example ŌĆö Instrumentation Amplifier Design

**Problem:** A strain-gauge bridge has output $\pm 20$ mV on a 2.5 V common-mode. An MCU ADC accepts 0ŌĆō3.3 V. Design an INA gain so the signal uses 80% of full scale at maximum strain.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute target peak output.**

$$
V_{\text{out, max}} = 0.8 \times 3.3 = 2.64 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute required gain.**

$$
G = \frac{2.64 \text{ V}}{0.020 \text{ V}} = 132
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Choose a standard INA (e.g., INA128) and compute $R_g$.**

For the INA128, the gain equation is:

$$
G = 1 + \frac{50 \text{ k}\Omega}{R_g}
$$

Solving:

$$
R_g = \frac{50\,000}{G - 1} = \frac{50\,000}{131} \approx 381.7 \text{ }\Omega
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Round to nearest standard E96 value.**

$R_g \approx 383 \text{ }\Omega$ yields $G \approx 131.6$, giving $V_{\text{out,max}} \approx 2.632$ V ŌĆö well within the 3.3 V rail.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Anti-aliasing filter.**

Place a first-order RC low-pass with $f_c$ set to roughly 5├Ś the highest signal of interest and $\leq f_s / 2$. For $f_c = 1$ kHz with $C = 100$ nF:

$$
R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 1000 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 1591.5 \text{ }\Omega
$$

Use $R = 1.6 \text{ k}\Omega$ (E12 standard).

---

## 3.5 Sampling Rate Selection ŌĆö Numerical Demonstration

**Problem:** A mechatronic vision system uses a camera producing analog video at 30 Hz bandwidth. The MCU samples at 60 Hz. Will aliasing occur?

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply Nyquist.**

$$
f_s = 60 \text{ Hz}, \quad f_{\text{max}} = 30 \text{ Hz}
$$

$$
f_s < 2 f_{\text{max}} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Aliasing guaranteed}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Required minimum.**

$$
f_{s,\text{min}} = 2 \times 30 = 60 \text{ Hz (exactly Nyquist boundary)}
$$

For practical safety, set $f_s \geq 5 \times f_{\text{max}} = 150$ Hz with a steep anti-aliasing analog filter at 30 Hz.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Sampling at exactly $2 f_{\text{max}}$ is theoretically sufficient but requires an *ideal* brick-wall filter, which is unrealizable. **Always sample at 5├ŚŌĆō10├Ś the signal bandwidth.**

## Part A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]
**Differentiate between a microcontroller and a microprocessor. Why are microcontrollers preferred in mechatronic systems?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Microprocessor | Microcontroller |
|---|---|---|
| Memory & I/O | External to chip | Integrated on-chip |
| Cost | High | Low |
| Power consumption | High | Low |
| Application | General-purpose PC | Embedded dedicated control |
| Bit-width commonly | 32/64-bit | 8/16/32-bit |

Microcontrollers are preferred in mechatronic systems because they integrate the CPU, RAM, ROM, ADC, PWM, timers, and communication peripherals in **a single IC**, reducing size, cost, power, and design complexity. They are optimized for **real-time deterministic control** of mechanical systems (motors, valves, sensors), which is the core requirement of any mechatronic design.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]
**Explain the need for signal conditioning in a mechatronic measurement system. List four common signal conditioning operations.**

**Model Answer:**

A sensor output is often **too weak (mV), too noisy, non-linear, or in an incompatible range** to be fed directly into an ADC or MCU. Signal conditioning bridges this gap.

**Four common signal conditioning operations:**

1. **Amplification** ŌĆö Increasing the weak sensor signal (e.g., instrumentation amplifier for strain gauges).
2. **Filtering** ŌĆö Removing noise and preventing aliasing (e.g., low-pass RC, Sallen-Key).
3. **Linearization** ŌĆö Compensating for non-linear sensor response (e.g., thermistor curve fitting).
4. **Level Shifting / Isolation** ŌĆö Adapting voltage levels and providing galvanic isolation (opto-coupler, transformer).

### Question A ŌĆö Choice 1 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a typical microcontroller. Discuss the role of each peripheral in a mechatronic application. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A 12-bit ADC with reference voltage 3.3 V is used to digitize the output of a temperature sensor. The sensor output ranges from 0.5 V to 2.5 V. Find: (i) ADC resolution in mV, (ii) ADC code at the minimum and maximum sensor outputs. **[7 marks]**

#### Solution to (a):

A microcontroller has the following functional blocks:

- **CPU (ALU, Registers, Decoder):** Executes instructions and performs arithmetic/logic operations. In a robotic arm, the CPU runs the inverse-kinematics and PID control algorithms.
- **Program Memory (Flash):** Stores the firmware permanently. Holds the control laws even after power-off.
- **Data Memory (SRAM):** Stores run-time variables, stack, sensor readings. Used as a scratchpad during PID computations.
- **Clock Generator:** Provides the time base using an external crystal/oscillator. Determines instruction cycle time and PWM frequency.
- **GPIO:** Bit-level I/O to interface LEDs, switches, relays, and bit-banged protocols.
- **ADC:** Converts analog sensor signals to digital. Used to read potentiometer, LM35 temperature output, load cell bridge output.
- **PWM:** Generates variable duty-cycle pulses to drive DC motor speed, servo angle, LED brightness.
- **Timers/Counters:** Decode quadrature encoders, generate delays, debounce switches.
- **Communication (UART/I┬▓C/SPI/CAN):** Interact with other sensors, displays, and master controllers.

The peripheral roles together implement a **sensor ŌåÆ process ŌåÆ actuator** mechatronic pipeline.

#### Solution to (b):

**Given:** $n = 12$, $V_{\text{ref}} = 3.3$ V, sensor range $0.5$ V to $2.5$ V.

**(i) Resolution (LSB):**

$$
\text{LSB} = \frac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^n} = \frac{3.3}{4096} = 8.0566 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} \approx 0.8057 \text{ mV}
$$

**(ii) ADC codes:**

At $V_{\text{in}} = 0.5$ V:

$$
\text{Code}_{\min} = \left\lfloor \frac{0.5}{3.3} \times 4096 \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 620.6 \right\rfloor = 620
$$

At $V_{\text{in}} = 2.5$ V:

$$
\text{Code}_{\max} = \left\lfloor \frac{2.5}{3.3} \times 4096 \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 3103.0 \right\rfloor = 3103
$$

**Number of usable codes:** $3103 - 620 + 1 = 2484$ out of $4096$, meaning only ~60% of ADC range is used ŌĆö students may add a note on amplifying the sensor signal to use the full ADC range.

### Question B ŌĆö Choice 2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]

**(a)** With a suitable diagram, explain the working of an LVDT and derive the relationship between its output voltage and core displacement. List two advantages of LVDT over a potentiometer as a displacement sensor. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** An instrumentation amplifier has $R_1 = R_3 = 25 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = R_4 = 50 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_g = 1.2 \text{ k}\Omega$. A strain-gauge bridge provides a differential voltage of 15 mV on a 2.5 V common-mode. Compute (i) the gain of the amplifier, (ii) the output voltage. **[7 marks]**

#### Solution to (a):

**LVDT Working (Diagram in words):** A Linear Variable Differential Transformer consists of one primary winding and two secondary windings (S1 and S2) wound on a cylindrical former. A movable soft-iron core slides inside the former. The primary is excited by an AC source (typically 1ŌĆō10 kHz).

When the core is **at null position**, the EMFs induced in S1 and S2 are equal and opposite ŌåÆ net output is **zero**.

When the core moves **toward S1**, more flux links S1, so $|E_1| > |E_2|$. The differential output $E_0 = E_1 - E_2$ has a magnitude proportional to displacement and a phase that indicates direction.

When the core moves **toward S2**, the phase of $E_0$ reverses.

**Derivation:** Assuming the primary produces flux $\phi_p$, the induced EMFs in secondaries are:

$$
E_1 = -N_1 \frac{d\phi_1}{dt}, \quad E_2 = -N_2 \frac{d\phi_2}{dt}
$$

For equal turns $N_1 = N_2 = N$ and flux linearly dependent on displacement $x$:

$$
\phi_1 = k (x_0 + x), \quad \phi_2 = k (x_0 - x)
$$

Net output:

$$
E_0 = E_1 - E_2 = -N \frac{d}{dt}\left[\phi_1 - \phi_2\right] = -N \frac{d}{dt}(2k x) = -2Nk \frac{dx}{dt}
$$

The RMS output for sinusoidal displacement is:

$$
E_{0,\text{rms}} = 2Nk\omega X \sin(\omega t)
$$

For small displacement about null, the **amplitude** is proportional to $|x|$ and the **phase** indicates direction.

**Two Advantages over Potentiometer:**

1. **Frictionless and contactless** ŌåÆ no wear, infinite resolution in theory, longer life.
2. **High linearity** over a wide range and produces an AC output that can be remotely conditioned via long cables without offset errors.

#### Solution to (b):

**(i) Gain:** Standard three-op-amp instrumentation amplifier formula:

$$
G = \left(1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_g}\right) \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2}
$$

$$
G = \left(1 + \frac{2 \times 25\,000}{1\,200}\right) \cdot \frac{50\,000}{25\,000} = \left(1 + 41.667\right) \cdot 2 = 42.667 \cdot 2 = 85.33
$$

**(ii) Output Voltage:**

$$
V_{\text{out}} = G \cdot V_{\text{diff}} = 85.33 \times 0.015 = 1.28 \text{ V}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mistakes** in ADC resolution ŌĆö always write mV/V, not bare numbers.
> 2. **Confusing reference voltages** ŌĆö for ratiometric sensors, the bridge excitation equals $V_{\text{ref}}$; otherwise, treat as separate.
> 3. **Sign errors in INA** ŌĆö the gain formula has $\frac{2R_1}{R_g}$ inside the parenthesis, NOT $\frac{R_1}{R_g}$.
> 4. **Forgetting the floor() / truncation** in ADC code calculations ŌĆö codes are integers.
> 5. **Drawing the LVDT without labeling primary/secondary/null position** ŌĆö a labeled diagram fetches full marks; a bare rectangle loses 2 marks.
