## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Microcontroller** = CPU + memory + peripherals on a single chip ŌåÆ preferred for real-time mechatronic control due to integration, low cost, and deterministic behavior.
- Most modern MCUs use **modified Harvard** architecture (separate program/data buses) ŌĆö example families: 8051, PIC, AVR, ARM Cortex-M, ESP32.
- **Sensors** are classified as **passive (self-generating)** or **active (need excitation)** ŌĆö distinguish clearly in exams.
- Key equations to memorize:
  - Strain gauge: $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G_f \varepsilon$
  - LVDT: $E_0 \propto x$ with phase indicating direction
  - Thermocouple: $V = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$
  - Hall: $V_H = \dfrac{R_H I B}{t}$
- **Signal conditioning** = amplification + filtering + isolation + level shifting + linearization ŌĆö required because real sensor signals are weak, noisy, and incompatible with ADC input ranges.
- **Instrumentation amplifier** is mandatory for bridge sensors (strain gauge, RTD in bridge) ŌĆö gain formula $\left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}\right) \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$.
- **ADC resolution**: $\text{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^n}$ ; **SQNR** Ōēł $6.02n + 1.76$ dB; **Nyquist**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{\text{max}}$; **anti-aliasing filter** mandatory.
- **Interfacing protocols** ŌĆö know differences:
  - **UART** = asynchronous, point-to-point, 2 wires.
  - **I┬▓C** = 2-wire, multi-slave, addressable, slow.
  - **SPI** = 4-wire, fast, master-slave.
  - **CAN** = differential, robust, automotive.
- **Design flow** = sensor selection ŌåÆ signal conditioning ŌåÆ ADC ŌåÆ MCU algorithm ŌåÆ driver ŌåÆ actuator ŌåÆ feedback loop.
- Always mention **CMRR** when justifying use of INA.
- Always draw a **labeled block diagram** in long answers ŌĆö it is worth 2ŌĆō3 marks by itself.
- For numerical problems, show units at every step and round off only at the final answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modeling Techniques: Introduction to Mathematical, Physical, and Empirical Models; Modeling Mechanical, Electrical, and Control Subsystems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT861 - MECHATRONIC SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Modeling Techniques: Introduction to Mathematical, Physical, and Empirical Models; Modeling Mechanical, Electrical, and Control Subsystems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to System Modeling

**Modeling** is the foundational process in mechatronic system design where an engineer represents a real-world physical system using a simplified, mathematically tractable abstraction. According to KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, a *model* is a set of mathematical equations, logical relationships, or empirical relationships that describe the inputŌĆōoutput behavior of a system within a defined operating range.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PERAT861 ŌĆō Module 3):**
> Modeling is the process of producing a model which is a representation of the construction and working of some system of interest. A model is similar to but simpler than the system it represents. The simplification is typically done to make the system easier to understand, analyze, and design for a specific purpose.

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Modeling

#### A. Mathematical Model
A **mathematical model** uses a set of governing equations derived from fundamental physical laws (Newton's laws, Kirchhoff's laws, thermodynamic laws) to describe system behavior. It is **analytical**, **predictive**, and **generalizable** across operating ranges.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Real-World Analogy):** Think of a mathematical model as an *architect's blueprint* of a building. The blueprint does not physically exist, but it precisely captures the geometry, loads, and material behavior using equations and symbols. Similarly, a mathematical model captures the "geometry" of a system using differential equations.

#### B. Physical Model (Prototype / Scale Model)
A **physical model** is a tangible, scaled replica of the actual system. Engineers use it to validate designs, observe fluid flow, study structural stresses, or test aerodynamic properties. It is **empirical** in nature (results come from measurement, not derivation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Real-World Analogy):** A physical model is like a *toy replica of a car* that engineers put in a wind tunnel. It physically exists, looks similar to the real car, and produces real measurement data. Car manufacturers use clay models and wind-tunnel models long before the production car exists.

#### C. Empirical Model (Black-Box / Data-Driven Model)
An **empirical model** is built entirely from **experimental inputŌĆōoutput data** without any reference to the underlying physical laws. It uses curve-fitting, regression, or machine learning to relate inputs to outputs. It is **accurate only within the measured range**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Real-World Analogy):** An empirical model is like a *grandmother's recipe* for the perfect dish. She does not know food chemistry (no physical laws), but years of trial-and-error (data collection) have taught her the right temperature and time (inputŌĆōoutput relationship). The recipe works for her dish, but it will not work for a completely different dish.

### 1.3 Why Modeling is Indispensable in Mechatronics

| Aspect | Role of Modeling |
|---|---|
| **Analysis** | Predicts system response to inputs before fabrication. |
| **Design** | Enables selection of motors, sensors, controllers, and materials. |
| **Control** | Provides the transfer function needed for PID/fuzzy/state-space controller synthesis. |
| **Optimization** | Allows iterative improvement using simulation software (MATLAB/Simulink). |
| **Cost & Safety** | Reduces prototyping cost and prevents testing failures on expensive hardware. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Accuracy Metric:** A good model balances **fidelity** (closeness to reality) with **simplicity** (ease of analysis). The well-known rule of thumb is the **5ŌĆō10% steady-state error** tolerance in KTU board-exam acceptable answers.

---

### 1.4 The Mechatronic Modeling Hierarchy

A mechatronic system is inherently **multi-domain** ŌĆö it combines mechanical, electrical, electronic, and control sub-systems. To design such a system, we model each domain separately first, then integrate them using **energy-domain analogies** (forceŌĆōvoltage analogy or forceŌĆōcurrent analogy).

> [!TIP]
> **Key Term to Remember:** *Energy Domain* ŌĆö A physical domain (mechanical, electrical, fluid, thermal) characterized by two conjugate variables: an **across variable** (effort) and a **through variable** (flow). Power = Across ├Ś Through. This unified view is the bedrock of mechatronic modeling.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Modeling Process ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic

The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to follow this structured modeling workflow for any problem:

1. **System Identification** ŌĆö Define inputs, outputs, parameters, and boundaries.
2. **Assumption Listing** ŌĆö Idealize components (massless springs, frictionless surfaces, lumped elements).
3. **Law Selection** ŌĆö Pick the governing physical law (Newton's 2nd law, Kirchhoff's voltage/current law, etc.).
4. **Equation Formation** ŌĆö Write differential or algebraic equations.
5. **Domain Coupling** ŌĆö Link mechanical, electrical, and control sub-models through energy conversion elements (motors, sensors).
6. **Simplification** ŌĆö Linearize nonlinear terms (Taylor series around operating point) when possible.
7. **Validation** ŌĆö Compare model predictions with experimental data.

## 2.2 The Across-Through Variable Framework (Foundational Concept)

Every physical domain in mechatronics can be described by an **across variable (e)** and a **through variable (f)**. The product $e \times f$ yields power in **watts**.

| Domain | Across Variable (Effort) | Through Variable (Flow) | Energy-Storing Element |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanical (Translational) | Force $F$ [N] | Velocity $v$ [m/s] | Mass $M$ (stores kinetic) |
| Mechanical (Rotational) | Torque $T$ [N┬Ęm] | Angular velocity $\omega$ [rad/s] | Inertia $J$ (stores kinetic) |
| Electrical | Voltage $V$ [V] | Current $i$ [A] | Inductance $L$ (stores magnetic) |
| Fluid (Hydraulic) | Pressure $P$ [Pa] | Volumetric flow $Q$ [m┬│/s] | Fluid inertia $I_f$ |
| Thermal | Temperature $T$ [K] | Heat flow $\dot{Q}$ [W] | Thermal capacitance $C_{th}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The across-through framework allows a **mechanical system to be modeled as an equivalent electrical circuit** using the forceŌĆōvoltage analogy. This is the single most frequently asked modeling question in the KTU board exam.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆö Mechanical, Electrical & Control Subsystems

### A. Translational Mechanical Elements

| Element | Constitutive Equation | Energy Domain | Parameter | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mass $M$ | $F = M \dfrac{dv}{dt} = M\ddot{x}$ | Storage (kinetic) | $M$ | kg |
| Damper $B$ | $F = B\dfrac{dx}{dt} = Bv$ | Dissipation (friction) | $B$ | N┬Ęs/m |
| Spring $K$ | $F = Kx$ | Storage (potential) | $K$ | N/m |
| Friction $F_f$ | $F_f = \mu N$ (Coulomb) | Dissipation | $\mu$ | dimensionless |

### B. Rotational Mechanical Elements

| Element | Constitutive Equation | Parameter | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inertia $J$ | $T = J\dfrac{d\omega}{dt} = J\ddot{\theta}$ | $J$ | kg┬Ęm┬▓ |
| Rotational Damper | $T = B_r \omega$ | $B_r$ | N┬Ęm┬Ęs/rad |
| Torsional Spring | $T = K_t \theta$ | $K_t$ | N┬Ęm/rad |

### C. Electrical Elements

| Element | Constitutive Equation | Parameter | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Resistor $R$ | $v = iR$ | $R$ | $\Omega$ |
| Inductor $L$ | $v = L\dfrac{di}{dt}$ | $L$ | H |
| Capacitor $C$ | $i = C\dfrac{dv}{dt}$ | $C$ | F |

### D. Gear Train Model

A gear train with teeth ratio $N = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ couples two shafts and transforms both torque and inertia.

$$T_2 = N \cdot T_1 \qquad \omega_2 = \dfrac{\omega_1}{N}$$

Reflected (equivalent) inertia and damping at the input shaft:

$$J_{eq} = J_1 + N^2 J_2 \qquad B_{eq} = B_1 + N^2 B_2$$

### E. DC Motor Model (Electro-Mechanical Coupling)

| Equation | Description |
|---|---|
| $V = Ri + L\dfrac{di}{dt} + K_b \omega$ | Electrical (armature) side |
| $T = K_t i$ | Electromechanical coupling |
| $T = J\dfrac{d\omega}{dt} + B\omega + T_L$ | Mechanical (rotor) side |
| $\dfrac{\omega(s)}{V(s)} = \dfrac{K_t}{(Ls+R)(Js+B) + K_t K_b}$ | Open-loop transfer function |

### F. Control Subsystem ŌĆö Transfer Function & Block Diagram Rules

| Rule | Formula / Operation |
|---|---|
| Series (cascade) | $G(s) = G_1(s) \cdot G_2(s)$ |
| Parallel | $G(s) = G_1(s) + G_2(s)$ |
| Negative feedback closed-loop | $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ |
| Positive feedback closed-loop | $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 - G(s)H(s)}$ |
| Moving a block past a summing junction | Use $G/(1\pm GH)$ inversion rules |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Test Signals (used in control modeling):**
> - **Step input:** $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$
> - **Ramp input:** $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s^2}$
> - **Impulse input:** $R(s) = 1$
> - **Parabolic input:** $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s^3}$

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Models

- **Automotive:** Mass-spring-damper models are used in active suspension design to balance ride comfort and road holding.
- **Robotics:** DC motor models enable precise trajectory planning in robotic arms.
- **Aerospace:** RLC equivalent circuits model structural vibrations of aircraft wings.
- **Industrial Automation:** Gear-train models predict motor sizing and gearbox selection.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: MassŌĆōSpringŌĆōDamper (Translational) System

**System Description:** A cart of mass $M$ is connected to a fixed wall through a spring of stiffness $K$ and a viscous damper of damping coefficient $B$. An external force $F(t)$ is applied horizontally. The cart displacement from equilibrium is $x(t)$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Free-Body Diagram (qualitative description):**
Forces acting on the mass $M$ along the positive $x$-direction:
- External applied force: $+F(t)$
- Spring restoring force: $-Kx$ (opposes displacement)
- Viscous damper force: $-B\dfrac{dx}{dt}$ (opposes velocity)

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply Newton's Second Law of Motion:**
The sum of forces equals mass times acceleration.

$$\sum F = M a = M \dfrac{d^2 x}{dt^2}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute the three forces into Newton's equation:**

$$F(t) - B\dfrac{dx}{dt} - Kx = M \dfrac{d^2 x}{dt^2}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Rearrange to standard form (highest derivative on the left):**

$$M \dfrac{d^2 x}{dt^2} + B \dfrac{dx}{dt} + K x = F(t)$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Convert to the Laplace Domain (zero initial conditions):**
Recall the Laplace transform: $\mathcal{L}\{x(t)\} = X(s)$, $\mathcal{L}\{\dot{x}(t)\} = sX(s)$, $\mathcal{L}\{\ddot{x}(t)\} = s^2 X(s)$.

$$Ms^2 X(s) + Bs X(s) + K X(s) = F(s)$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Factor out $X(s)$ and derive the Transfer Function:**

$$X(s)\left[Ms^2 + Bs + K\right] = F(s)$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{X(s)}{F(s)} = \dfrac{1}{Ms^2 + Bs + K}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- The denominator $Ms^2 + Bs + K = 0$ is the **characteristic equation** whose roots are the system poles.
- The **natural (undamped) frequency** is $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{M}}$ [rad/s].
- The **damping ratio** is $\zeta = \dfrac{B}{2\sqrt{MK}}$ (dimensionless).
- The **damping time constant** is $\tau = \dfrac{2M}{B}$ [s].

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Series RLC Electrical Circuit (Control Subsystem Foundation)

**System Description:** A series circuit has a resistor $R$, inductor $L$, and capacitor $C$ driven by an input voltage $v_{in}(t)$. The output is the voltage across the capacitor, $v_{out}(t)$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL):**
Sum of voltage drops around the loop equals the source voltage.

$$v_{in}(t) = v_R(t) + v_L(t) + v_C(t)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Write the element equations using the common current $i(t)$:**

$$v_R = iR \qquad v_L = L\dfrac{di}{dt} \qquad v_C = \dfrac{1}{C}\int i \, dt$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Express current in terms of capacitor voltage:**
Since $i = C\dfrac{dv_C}{dt}$ and $v_{out} = v_C$:

$$\dfrac{di}{dt} = C\dfrac{d^2 v_C}{dt^2}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute into the KVL equation:**

$$v_{in}(t) = RC\dfrac{dv_C}{dt} + LC\dfrac{d^2 v_C}{dt^2} + v_C$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Rearrange to standard form:**

$$LC\dfrac{d^2 v_{out}}{dt^2} + RC\dfrac{dv_{out}}{dt} + v_{out} = v_{in}(t)$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Take the Laplace Transform (zero initial conditions):**

$$LCs^2 V_{out}(s) + RCs V_{out}(s) + V_{out}(s) = V_{in}(s)$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Derive the Transfer Function:**

$$V_{out}(s) \left[LCs^2 + RCs + 1\right] = V_{in}(s)$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \dfrac{1}{LCs^2 + RCs + 1}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- The series RLC circuit is the **electrical analog** of the mass-spring-damper system.
- Compare coefficients with the mechanical equation: $M \leftrightarrow L$, $B \leftrightarrow R$, $K \leftrightarrow 1/C$.
- This forms the basis of the **forceŌĆōvoltage analogy** (also called the impedance analogy).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Gear Train ŌĆö Reflected Inertia Model

**System Description:** Two gears with $N_1$ and $N_2$ teeth couple a motor (input, shaft 1) to a load (output, shaft 2). The gear ratio is $n = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Conservation of Power (ideal, lossless gear):**

$$T_1 \omega_1 = T_2 \omega_2$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Geometric relationship of teeth and angular velocity:**

$$\omega_2 = \omega_1 \cdot \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{\omega_1}{n}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Express the load torque in terms of motor torque:**

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot \dfrac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = T_1 \cdot n$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Kinetic energy of the rotating load referred to shaft 1:**

$$KE_2 = \dfrac{1}{2} J_2 \omega_2^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \left(\dfrac{J_2}{n^2}\right) \omega_1^2$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Equate to equivalent inertia on shaft 1:**

$$\dfrac{1}{2} J_{eq} \omega_1^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \left(\dfrac{J_2}{n^2}\right) \omega_1^2$$

$$J_{eq} = \dfrac{J_2}{n^2}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Add the motor's own inertia to get the total equivalent inertia on shaft 1:**

$$\boxed{J_{total,\,eq} = J_1 + \dfrac{J_2}{n^2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General Rule:** Reflecting a rotational element from the *load side* to the *motor side* divides the inertia (or damping) by $n^2$. Reflecting from motor side to load side multiplies it by $n^2$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Block Diagram Reduction ŌĆö Unity Negative Feedback

**System Description:** A forward path block $G(s)$ with a feedback path block $H(s)$ in a negative feedback configuration.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the error signal equation:**

$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s) C(s)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Write the output equation:**

$$C(s) = G(s) E(s) = G(s)\left[R(s) - H(s)C(s)\right]$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Expand and group $C(s)$ terms:**

$$C(s) = G(s) R(s) - G(s) H(s) C(s)$$

$$C(s) + G(s) H(s) C(s) = G(s) R(s)$$

$$C(s)\left[1 + G(s) H(s)\right] = G(s) R(s)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Solve for the closed-loop transfer function $T(s) = \dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)}$:**

$$\boxed{T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Case ŌĆö Unity Feedback:** When $H(s) = 1$, the formula reduces to $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$, which is the most common form tested in the KTU board exam.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix ŌĆö Mechatronic Domain Equivalence

| Mechanical (Translational) | Mechanical (Rotational) | Electrical (Series) | Energy Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| Force $F$ | Torque $T$ | Voltage $V$ | Effort (Across) |
| Velocity $v$ | Angular velocity $\omega$ | Current $i$ | Flow (Through) |
| Mass $M$ | Inertia $J$ | Inductance $L$ | Kinetic storage |
| Damper $B$ | Rotational damper $B_r$ | Resistance $R$ | Dissipation |
| Spring $K$ | Torsional spring $K_t$ | $1/C$ (inverse capacitance) | Potential storage |
| Displacement $x$ | Angular displacement $\theta$ | Charge $q$ | Integral of flow |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Modeling Process Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the KTU 2024 scheme modeling workflow, from physical system to validated analytical model.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Physical System Real World Problem] --> A1[Step 1 System Identification and Boundary Definition]
    A1 --> A2[Step 2 Assumption Listing Idealizations]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3 Apply Governing Physical Laws]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4 Form Differential or Algebraic Equations]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5 Domain Coupling Sensors and Actuators]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6 Linearization and Simplification]
    A6 --> A7[Step 7 Simulation and Validation]
    A7 --> A8[Validated Analytical Model Ready for Controller Design]
    A8 --> A9[Part A Mechanical Model]
    A8 --> A10[Part B Electrical Model]
    A8 --> A11[Part C Control Subsystem Model]
```

---

## 4.2 MassŌĆōSpringŌĆōDamper Schematic (Free-Body to Transfer Function)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Fixed Wall] --> A1[Spring K]
    A1 --> A2[Mass M Block]
    A2 --> A3[Damper B to Ground]
    A0 --> A4[External Force F applied horizontally to M]
    A4 --> A2
    A2 --> A5[Output Displacement x]
    A2 --> A6[Newton Second Law Application]
    A6 --> A7[Differential Equation Mx_ddot plus Bx_dot plus Kx equals F]
    A7 --> A8[Laplace Transform Zero Initial Conditions]
    A8 --> A9[Transfer Function X_s over F_s equals one over Ms_squared plus Bs plus K]
```

---

## 4.3 Series RLC Circuit (Schematic Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B0[Input Voltage V_in] --> B1[Resistor R]
    B1 --> B2[Inductor L]
    B2 --> B3[Capacitor C]
    B3 --> B4[Output Voltage V_out across C]
    B4 --> B5[Ground Node Return Path]
    B5 --> B0
    B0 --> B6[Apply KVL around the loop]
    B6 --> B7[Element equations v_R equals iR, v_L equals L di_dt, v_C equals one over C integral i dt]
    B7 --> B8[Substitute current i equals C dv_C_dt]
    B8 --> B9[Second order ODE LC v_ddot plus RC v_dot plus v equals V_in]
    B9 --> B10[Transfer Function V_out over V_in equals one over LCs_squared plus RCs plus one]
```

---

## 4.4 ForceŌĆōVoltage Analogy (Domain Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0[Mechanical Translational System] --> C1[Force F analogous to Voltage V]
    C0 --> C2[Velocity v analogous to Current i]
    C0 --> C3[Mass M analogous to Inductance L]
    C0 --> C4[Damper B analogous to Resistance R]
    C0 --> C5[Spring K analogous to Inverse Capacitance one over C]
    C1 --> C6[Unified Across Through Framework]
    C2 --> C6
    C3 --> C6
    C4 --> C6
    C5 --> C6
    C6 --> C7[Single Domain Model for Control Design]
    C7 --> C8[Transfer Function in s domain]
    C8 --> C9[Block Diagram Representation]
    C9 --> C10[Controller Synthesis PID State Space]
```

---

## 4.5 Control Subsystem ŌĆö Closed-Loop Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D0[Reference Input R_s] --> D1[Summing Junction negative feedback]
    D1 --> D2[Error Signal E_s]
    D2 --> D3[Controller Gc_s]
    D3 --> D4[Plant Gp_s]
    D4 --> D5[Output C_s]
    D5 --> D6[Feedback Sensor H_s]
    D6 --> D1
    D5 --> D7[Closed Loop Transfer Function T_s equals Gc times Gp over 1 plus Gc times Gp times H]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Diagram Matters in KTU Exams:** The block diagram reduction rules (Section 2, part F) are applied to these standard configurations. Students who can identify series, parallel, and feedback paths instantly score full marks on the 14-mark Part B questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
Differentiate between **physical models** and **empirical models** in mechatronic system design. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Physical Model | Empirical Model |
|---|---|---|
| **Basis** | Tangible, scaled replica of the actual system | Built from inputŌĆōoutput experimental data |
| **Derivation** | Constructed using similarity laws (geometric, kinematic, dynamic) | Derived using regression, curve-fitting, or neural networks |
| **Underlying Laws** | Does not use governing equations; relies on measurement | Does not use governing laws; relies on data |
| **Accuracy** | Limited by scaling and material differences | Limited to the operating range of training data |
| **Example** | Wind-tunnel model of an aircraft wing | Polynomial fit of motor speed vs. voltage from lab tests |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** State the **definition in one line** then give a **contrasting table** ŌĆö this guarantees 3/3 marks.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, Model Paper]**
State and explain the **forceŌĆōvoltage analogy** used in mechatronic modeling. List the mechanicalŌĆōelectrical equivalents for mass, damper, and spring.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **forceŌĆōvoltage analogy** (also called the **impedance analogy**) is a modeling technique that maps a translational mechanical system onto an equivalent series electrical circuit, allowing the well-developed tools of circuit analysis to be applied to mechanical problems.

Equivalents (forceŌĆōvoltage mapping):

| Mechanical | Electrical Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Force $F$ [N] | Voltage $V$ [V] |
| Velocity $v$ [m/s] | Current $i$ [A] |
| Mass $M$ [kg] | Inductance $L$ [H] |
| Damper $B$ [N┬Ęs/m] | Resistance $R$ [$\Omega$] |
| Spring $K$ [N/m] | Inverse Capacitance $1/C$ [1/F] |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** A diagram showing the analogy (mass Ōåö inductor, spring Ōåö capacitor inverse) earns a **bonus half mark** in many valuation schemes.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A ŌĆö Full Model Question

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Modified]**
**(a)** Derive the mathematical model (transfer function) of a **massŌĆōspringŌĆōdamper translational mechanical system** subjected to an external force $F(t)$. Clearly state the assumptions and define all variables. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An RLC series electrical circuit is to be modeled as an equivalent mechanical system. Using the forceŌĆōvoltage analogy, derive the transfer function and identify the equivalent mass, damping, and stiffness. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

##### Part (a) ŌĆö Mechanical Model Derivation (7 Marks)

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
- The mass $M$ is a rigid body (no deformation).
- The spring is linear (force proportional to displacement) with stiffness $K$.
- The damper is viscous (force proportional to velocity) with coefficient $B$.
- Motion is one-dimensional along the horizontal $x$-axis.
- Initial conditions are zero.

**Free-Body Diagram (1 mark):** Three forces on mass $M$: applied force $+F(t)$ (right), spring force $-Kx$ (left), damper force $-B\dot{x}$ (left).

**Apply Newton's Second Law (2 marks):**

$$F(t) - B\dot{x} - Kx = M\ddot{x}$$

Rearrange to standard form:

$$M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + Kx = F(t)$$

**Laplace Transform (1 mark):** With zero initial conditions, $\ddot{x} \rightarrow s^2 X(s)$ and $\dot{x} \rightarrow s X(s)$.

$$Ms^2 X(s) + Bs X(s) + K X(s) = F(s)$$

**Transfer Function (2 marks):** Factor out $X(s)$ and solve.

$$\boxed{\dfrac{X(s)}{F(s)} = \dfrac{1}{Ms^2 + Bs + K}}$$

**Engineering Insights (optional, for full marks):**
- Natural frequency $\omega_n = \sqrt{K/M}$ [rad/s]
- Damping ratio $\zeta = \dfrac{B}{2\sqrt{MK}}$ (dimensionless)

---

##### Part (b) ŌĆö Electrical-to-Mechanical Equivalent (7 Marks)

**Step 1 ŌĆö KVL around the series RLC loop (1 mark):**

$$v_{in}(t) = v_R + v_L + v_C = iR + L\dfrac{di}{dt} + \dfrac{1}{C}\int i \, dt$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Express current in terms of capacitor voltage (1 mark):**

$$i = C\dfrac{dv_C}{dt} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{di}{dt} = C\dfrac{d^2 v_C}{dt^2}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute and rearrange (2 marks):**

$$LC\dfrac{d^2 v_{out}}{dt^2} + RC\dfrac{dv_{out}}{dt} + v_{out} = v_{in}(t)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Laplace Transform and transfer function (1 mark):**

$$\dfrac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \dfrac{1}{LCs^2 + RCs + 1}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Apply the forceŌĆōvoltage analogy (2 marks):**
Match coefficients with the mechanical equation $Ms^2 + Bs + K$:

| Mechanical | Electrical | Identification |
|---|---|---|
| $M$ | $L$ | Equivalent mass = $L$ |
| $B$ | $R$ | Equivalent damping = $R$ |
| $K$ | $1/C$ | Equivalent stiffness = $1/C$ |

$$\boxed{\text{Mechanical equivalent transfer function: } \dfrac{X(s)}{F(s)} = \dfrac{1}{Ms^2 + Bs + K} = \dfrac{1}{Ls^2 + Rs + \tfrac{1}{C}}}$$

---

### Question B ŌĆö Alternative Full Model Question

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, Modified]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, derive the **closed-loop transfer function** of a unity negative feedback control system with forward path gain $G(s)$ and feedback path $H(s)$. State the standard formula. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A DC motor drives a load through a gear train with ratio $N = 5$. The motor armature has $R = 2\,\Omega$, $L = 0.5\,\text{H}$, inertia $J_m = 0.01\,\text{kg┬Ęm}^2$, and damping $B_m = 0.001\,\text{N┬Ęm┬Ęs/rad}$. The load has inertia $J_L = 0.8\,\text{kg┬Ęm}^2$ and damping $B_L = 0.05\,\text{N┬Ęm┬Ęs/rad}$. Compute the **equivalent inertia and damping referred to the motor shaft**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

##### Part (a) ŌĆö Closed-Loop Transfer Function (7 Marks)

**Block Diagram Description (1 mark):** A reference $R(s)$ enters a summing junction (with negative feedback from $H(s)$), producing error $E(s) = R(s) - H(s) C(s)$. The error passes through $G(s)$ to produce the output $C(s)$. The output is fed back through $H(s)$ to the summing junction.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Error equation (1 mark):**

$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s) C(s)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Forward-path output equation (1 mark):**

$$C(s) = G(s) E(s)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute and expand (2 marks):**

$$C(s) = G(s) R(s) - G(s) H(s) C(s)$$

$$C(s)\left[1 + G(s) H(s)\right] = G(s) R(s)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Closed-loop transfer function (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{T(s) = \dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Special case for unity feedback $H(s) = 1$ (1 mark):**

$$T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Condition for stability:** The poles of $T(s)$ (roots of $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$) must lie in the left half of the $s$-plane. This is the **characteristic equation** used in RouthŌĆōHurwitz stability analysis.

---

##### Part (b) ŌĆö Gear Train Equivalent Inertia (7 Marks)

**Given Data (1 mark):**
- Gear ratio $N = N_1/N_2 = 5$ (or $n = N_2/N_1 = 5$ depending on convention; here we take the load gear is 5├Ś the motor gear teeth, so $N_2/N_1 = 5$)
- $J_m = 0.01\,\text{kg┬Ęm}^2$, $B_m = 0.001\,\text{N┬Ęm┬Ęs/rad}$
- $J_L = 0.8\,\text{kg┬Ęm}^2$, $B_L = 0.05\,\text{N┬Ęm┬Ęs/rad}$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the ratio $n = N_2/N_1 = 5$ (1 mark):**

**Step 2 ŌĆö Reflect load inertia to motor shaft (2 marks):**
Reflecting from the load side to the motor side divides by $n^2$.

$$J_{L,\,ref} = \dfrac{J_L}{n^2} = \dfrac{0.8}{(5)^2} = \dfrac{0.8}{25} = 0.032\,\text{kg┬Ęm}^2$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Add motor's own inertia to get total equivalent inertia (1 mark):**

$$J_{eq} = J_m + J_{L,\,ref} = 0.01 + 0.032 = 0.042\,\text{kg┬Ęm}^2$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Reflect load damping to motor shaft (1 mark):**

$$B_{L,\,ref} = \dfrac{B_L}{n^2} = \dfrac{0.05}{25} = 0.002\,\text{N┬Ęm┬Ęs/rad}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Total equivalent damping (1 mark):**

$$B_{eq} = B_m + B_{L,\,ref} = 0.001 + 0.002 = 0.003\,\text{N┬Ęm┬Ęs/rad}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{J_{eq} = 0.042\,\text{kg┬Ęm}^2 \qquad B_{eq} = 0.003\,\text{N┬Ęm┬Ęs/rad}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A high gear ratio ($n \gg 1$) dramatically reduces the load's reflected inertia to the motor, making the system respond faster. However, it also multiplies the load torque at the motor by $n$, requiring a higher-torque motor.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in the KTU Board Exam:**
> 1. **Confusing the gear ratio direction:** Students often write $J_{eq} = J_1 + N^2 J_2$ when the correct formula (reflecting *to the motor*) is $J_{eq} = J_1 + J_2/N^2$. Carefully read whether you are reflecting *to the load* (multiply by $N^2$) or *to the motor* (divide by $N^2$). ŌĆö **Lose up to 2 marks.**
> 2. **Forgetting to state assumptions:** The KTU valuation key explicitly awards **1 mark for listing assumptions** (rigid body, linear spring, viscous damping, 1-D motion, zero initial conditions). Skipping this loses a free mark.
> 3. **Missing the Laplace Transform step:** Students often write the differential equation and jump straight to the transfer function. You **must** show the Laplace substitution step ($d/dt \to s$, $d^2/dt^2 \to s^2$) to earn the intermediate marks.
> 4. **Sign errors in Newton's law:** Spring and damper forces must be negative (restoring) when displacement is positive. A positive sign in the differential equation indicates a wrong FBD and **loses 2ŌĆō3 marks**.
> 5. **Mixing up the analogies:** ForceŌĆōvoltage and forceŌĆōcurrent analogies give **different** electrical equivalents. Memorize both clearly:
>    - *ForceŌĆōVoltage (Impedance):* $M \leftrightarrow L$, $B \leftrightarrow R$, $K \leftrightarrow 1/C$
>    - *ForceŌĆōCurrent (Mobility):* $M \leftrightarrow C$, $B \leftrightarrow 1/R$, $K \leftrightarrow 1/L$

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Modeling** is the process of representing a real system with a mathematical, physical, or empirical abstraction.
- **Mathematical models** are equation-based, derived from physical laws (Newton, Kirchhoff, etc.) ŌĆö *general and predictive*.
- **Physical models** are tangible, scaled replicas used for experimental validation ŌĆö *empirical and observation-based*.
- **Empirical models** are pure inputŌĆōoutput data fits with no governing laws ŌĆö *narrow but fast*.
- The **massŌĆōspringŌĆōdamper** transfer function is $\dfrac{X(s)}{F(s)} = \dfrac{1}{Ms^2 + Bs + K}$, with natural frequency $\omega_n = \sqrt{K/M}$ and damping ratio $\zeta = B/(2\sqrt{MK})$.
- The **series RLC** transfer function is $\dfrac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \dfrac{1}{LCs^2 + RCs + 1}$.
- The **forceŌĆōvoltage analogy** maps: $F \leftrightarrow V$, $v \leftrightarrow i$, $M \leftrightarrow L$, $B \leftrightarrow R$, $K \leftrightarrow 1/C$.
- **Gear train reflection** (to motor shaft): $J_{eq} = J_m + J_L/n^2$, $B_{eq} = B_m + B_L/n^2$.
- **Closed-loop transfer function** with negative feedback: $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$; with unity feedback ($H = 1$): $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$.
- **Stability rule:** All poles of the closed-loop transfer function must have **negative real parts** (lie in the left half of the $s$-plane).
- The **across-through framework** unifies mechanical, electrical, fluid, and thermal domains using effort ├Ś flow = power.
- Always **list assumptions** explicitly, show the **free-body diagram** or **KVL equation**, perform the **Laplace transform** step, and present the **final transfer function in a box**.
- **Units to memorize:** Force [N], Torque [N┬Ęm], $M$ [kg], $J$ [kg┬Ęm┬▓], $B$ [N┬Ęs/m or N┬Ęm┬Ęs/rad], $K$ [N/m or N┬Ęm/rad], $R$ [$\Omega$], $L$ [H], $C$ [F], $\omega_n$ [rad/s], $\zeta$ [dimensionless].

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simulation Tools: Hands-on Experience with MATLAB/Simulink and LabVIEW; Simulating Mechatronic Systems to Analyze Performance and Behavior
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT861 - MECHATRONIC SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Simulation Tools: Hands-on Experience with MATLAB/Simulink and LabVIEW; Simulating Mechatronic Systems to Analyze Performance and Behavior

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Simulation Tools** in mechatronic system design refer to sophisticated, multi-domain, computer-aided software environments that allow engineers to mathematically model, numerically solve, visually represent, and dynamically analyze the coupled behavior of integrated mechanical, electronic, control, and software subsystems *before* physical prototyping. As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)**, the two industry-standard platforms explicitly mandated for hands-on competence are:

1. **MATLAB / Simulink** (MathWorks): A matrix-oriented numerical computing environment coupled with a graphical block-diagram simulation engine based on *time-domain differential equation solvers*.
2. **LabVIEW** (National Instruments): A *graphical programming* (G-language) dataflow-based development environment used for instrument control, signal acquisition, and virtual instrumentation (VI) prototyping.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate (Module 3):** Students are required to develop *operational fluency* ŌĆö meaning the ability to *model, run, parameterize, and interpret* simulation results of a mechatronic system (e.g., DC motor, thermal plant, or inverted pendulum) using at least one of these tools. Mere knowledge of menu options is **not** sufficient for full marks.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö "The Flight Simulator for Engineers"**
> Think of MATLAB/Simulink and LabVIEW as the engineering equivalent of a **flight simulator** used to train pilots. A pilot does not learn to fly a Boeing 737 by trial-and-error on a real aircraft (which would be catastrophic). Instead, he practices in a high-fidelity simulator that mimics aerodynamics, weather, and engine behavior. Similarly:
> - The **mechatronic system** is the "aircraft" (motor + sensor + controller + gearbox).
> - The **simulation tool** is the "cockpit simulator" ŌĆö it mimics the physics.
> - The **mathematical model** (transfer functions, state-space) is the "aerodynamic code" inside the simulator.
> - The **student/engineer** is the "pilot," learning the *response* of the system to *inputs* (step, ramp, impulse) before risking real hardware, time, or money.

The principal objective is **Virtual Prototyping** ŌĆö predicting **Transient Response** (rise time, settling time, overshoot) and **Steady-State Behavior** (steady-state error) entirely inside the computer.

### 1.3 Why Simulation is Indispensable in Mechatronics

| Engineering Driver | Simulation Benefit |
|---|---|
| Cost of physical prototype | **Reduces** by 60ŌĆō80% via virtual iteration |
| Safety (high-voltage/high-speed systems) | Enables **zero-risk** testing |
| Design space exploration | Allows **sweeping** parameters (Kp, Ki, Kd) rapidly |
| Multi-domain coupling | Single tool handles **mechanical + electrical + control** simultaneously |
| Time compression | A 10-hour physical test runs in **seconds** on a desktop |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Metric to Remember:**
> - **Accuracy:** Model fidelity vs. real plant.
> - **Precision:** Repeatability of simulation runs.
> - **Convergence:** Stability of the numerical solver (Runge-Kutta 4th order is the KTU-recommended default for stiff/non-stiff mechatronic ODEs).
> - **Real-Time Factor (RTF):** Ratio of simulation time to wall-clock time. RTF Ōēź 1 implies *faster-than-real-time* simulation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Workflow of Simulation (Step-by-Step Logic)

The simulation of any mechatronic system follows a **canonical 6-stage pipeline** which is repeatedly examined in KTU ESE questions:

1. **System Identification (SI):** Define inputs $\left\{u(t)\right\}$, outputs $\left\{y(t)\right\}$, and disturbance signals $\left\{d(t)\right\}$.
2. **Mathematical Modeling:** Derive governing equations. For a typical mechatronic subsystem (DC motor, thermal tank, hydraulic piston), the model is a **set of coupled first-order ODEs**.
3. **Representation in Tool-Specific Form:**
   - MATLAB: Transfer function $\dfrac{Y(s)}{U(s)}$ or state-space $(A, B, C, D)$.
   - Simulink: Block-diagram interconnection of integrators, gains, and summing junctions.
   - LabVIEW: Block diagram of Virtual Instrument (VI) with While-Loops, MathScript nodes, and DAQmx I/O.
4. **Parameterization:** Insert numerical values (resistance, inertia, damping, inductance).
5. **Solver Configuration:** Choose fixed-step vs. variable-step; set tolerance (e.g., $1 \times 10^{-6}$).
6. **Post-Processing & Validation:** Plot $y(t)$, compute performance indices, compare with analytical (hand-calculated) results.

### 2.2 The Two Foundational Mathematical Representations

#### A. Transfer Function Representation (S-Domain)

For a single-input single-output (SISO) linear time-invariant (LTI) mechatronic plant:

$$
G(s) \;=\; \frac{Y(s)}{U(s)} \;=\; \frac{b_{m} s^{m} + b_{m-1} s^{m-1} + \cdots + b_{1} s + b_{0}}{a_{n} s^{n} + a_{n-1} s^{n-1} + \cdots + a_{1} s + a_{0}}
$$

**Why?** The transfer function *converts* intractable differential equations into algebraic polynomials in the complex variable $s = \sigma + j\omega$, enabling the use of Laplace-transform techniques. It is the **default** input for `tf()` in MATLAB.

#### B. State-Space Representation (Time Domain)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) &= A\,\mathbf{x}(t) + B\,\mathbf{u}(t) \\
\mathbf{y}(t) &= C\,\mathbf{x}(t) + D\,\mathbf{u}(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why?** State-space is **superior** for multi-input multi-output (MIMO) systems, non-linear systems, and modern control design (LQR, pole-placement). MATLAB commands: `ss(A,B,C,D)` and `ss(G)`.

### 2.3 Numerical Solvers ŌĆö The "Engine" of Simulation

The ODEs of a mechatronic system are rarely solvable in closed form. Hence, the tool invokes a **numerical integrator**:

| Solver | Order | Use Case in Mechatronics |
|---|---|---|
| Euler (Forward) | 1st | Pedagogical, unstable for stiff systems |
| Runge-Kutta 4 (RK4) | 4th | **Default KTU recommendation** ŌĆö excellent for DC motors, position servos |
| ode45 (Dormand-Prince) | 4/5 adaptive | MATLAB default ŌĆö variable step |
| ode15s | Variable | **Stiff** systems (e.g., electrical circuits with $L \ll R$) |
| Backward Euler | 1st (implicit) | Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) real-time targets |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following equations verbatim ŌĆö they are direct KTU exam favorites.**

| Concept | Equation | Symbol Meaning | KTU Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Step Response of 1st Order | $y(t) = K\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | $K$ = DC gain, $\tau$ = time constant | $\tau$ in seconds (s) |
| Step Response of 2nd Order | $y(t) = 1 - \frac{e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \sin\left(\omega_d t + \phi\right)$ | $\zeta$ = damping ratio, $\omega_n$ = natural freq., $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | $\omega_n$ in rad/s |
| Rise Time (approx.) | $t_r \approx \frac{1.8}{\omega_n}$ | $0\% \rightarrow 100\%$ for 2nd order | **s** |
| Settling Time (2% criterion) | $t_s \approx \frac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$ | $98\%$ of final value | **s** |
| Peak Overshoot | $M_p = e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | **%** |
| Steady-State Error (Unity FB) | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_p}$ | $K_p$ = position error constant | Dimensionless |
| DC Motor Transfer Function | $G(s) = \dfrac{\theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \dfrac{K_m}{s\left[\left(L_a s + R_a\right)\left(J s + B\right) + K_b K_m\right]}$ | $K_m, K_b$ = motor & back-emf constants | $\theta$ in rad |
| Thermal System TF | $G(s) = \dfrac{T(s)}{Q(s)} = \dfrac{1}{C s + 1/R}$ | $C$ = thermal capacitance, $R$ = thermal resistance | $T$ in $^\circ$C |

> **NOTE on absolute value:** Use $\vert x \vert$ (rendered as `\vert x \vert`) ŌĆö never bare pipes inside the markdown table.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Automotive:** Simulating active suspension, ABS braking, engine ECU before road test.
- **Robotics:** Verifying trajectory tracking, inverse kinematics of a 6-DOF arm.
- **Aerospace:** Flight control law validation in Simulink (certifiable to DO-178C).
- **Industrial Automation:** PLC program virtual commissioning using LabVIEW + Factory I/O.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: DC Motor Position Servo Transfer Function

**Problem (Classic KTU Module 3 Derivative):** Derive the transfer function $\dfrac{\theta(s)}{V_a(s)}$ for a separately excited DC motor driving an inertial load, given armature voltage $V_a$, armature inductance $L_a$, armature resistance $R_a$, motor constant $K_m$, back-emf constant $K_b$, rotor inertia $J$, and viscous damping $B$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the Electrical (Armature) Loop Equation using Kirchhoff's Voltage Law:**

$$
V_a(t) \;=\; R_a \, i_a(t) \;+\; L_a \,\frac{di_a(t)}{dt} \;+\; e_b(t)
$$

**Explanation of terms:** The applied voltage $V_a$ must overcome three drops: the resistive drop $R_a i_a$ (Ohm's law), the inductive drop $L_a \tfrac{di_a}{dt}$ (Faraday's law), and the back-EMF $e_b$ generated by the spinning rotor acting as a generator.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Express the Back-EMF in terms of Angular Velocity:**

$$
e_b(t) \;=\; K_b \,\omega(t) \;=\; K_b \,\frac{d\theta(t)}{dt}
$$

**Explanation:** Faraday's law for a DC machine gives back-EMF proportional to angular velocity $\omega$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Write the Mechanical (Torque) Equation using Newton's 2nd Law for Rotation:**

$$
J \,\frac{d\omega(t)}{dt} \;+\; B\,\omega(t) \;=\; T_m(t) \;=\; K_m \, i_a(t)
$$

**Explanation:** The electromagnetic torque $T_m$ produced is proportional to armature current (with constant $K_m$). This torque accelerates the inertia $J$ and overcomes viscous friction $B\omega$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Convert All Three Equations to the Laplace Domain (zero initial conditions):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_a(s) &= (R_a + L_a s)\, I_a(s) + K_b\, s\, \Theta(s) \\
T_m(s) &= K_m\, I_a(s) \\
T_m(s) &= (J s + B)\, s\, \Theta(s)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Each derivative $\tfrac{d}{dt}$ becomes multiplication by $s$, and each integral becomes division by $s$. The mechanical equation multiplies by $s$ on the right because $T_m = (Js+B)\omega = (Js+B)s\Theta$.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Eliminate the Internal Variable $I_a(s)$:**

From equation 2: $I_a(s) = \dfrac{T_m(s)}{K_m}$.

Substitute into equation 1:

$$
V_a(s) = (R_a + L_a s) \cdot \frac{T_m(s)}{K_m} + K_b s \Theta(s)
$$

Substitute $T_m(s) = (J s + B)\, s\, \Theta(s)$:

$$
V_a(s) = \left[\frac{R_a + L_a s}{K_m}\right] \cdot (J s + B) \, s\, \Theta(s) \;+\; K_b s \Theta(s)
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Group the $\Theta(s)$ terms (Algebraic Manipulation):**

$$
V_a(s) = s \Theta(s) \left\{\frac{(R_a + L_a s)(J s + B)}{K_m} + K_b \right\}
$$

Combine over common denominator $K_m$:

$$
V_a(s) = \frac{s \Theta(s)}{K_m} \left\{ (R_a + L_a s)(J s + B) + K_b K_m \right\}
$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Solve for the Transfer Function $\dfrac{\theta(s)}{V_a(s)}$:**

$$
G(s) \;=\; \frac{\Theta(s)}{V_a(s)} \;=\; \frac{K_m}{s \left[ (R_a + L_a s)(J s + B) + K_b K_m \right]}
$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Expand the Denominator (useful for KTU problems on time constants):**

$$
(R_a + L_a s)(J s + B) \;=\; L_a J s^2 + (L_a B + R_a J) s + R_a B
$$

Thus the characteristic polynomial becomes:

$$
s \left[ L_a J s^2 + (L_a B + R_a J) s + (R_a B + K_b K_m) \right]
$$

This is a **3rd-order system** ŌĆö the open-loop DC motor has one integrator (from $\theta$ to $\omega$) plus two electrical/mechanical poles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Simplification Frequently Used in KTU Papers:** If $L_a$ is neglected (armature inductance is small, valid for low-frequency analysis), the system **reduces to 2nd order**, and the transfer function simplifies to:
> $$G(s) \;\approx\; \frac{K_m / [R_a B + K_b K_m]}{s \left( \tau_m s + 1 \right)}$$
> where the **mechanical time constant** is:
> $$\tau_m = \frac{R_a J}{R_a B + K_b K_m}$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Performance Specification Mapping

After simulation, the engineer must extract these standard KTU performance indices. The matrix below shows *how* each is computed in MATLAB/Simulink post-processing:

| Performance Index | Mathematical Definition | MATLAB/Simulink Command | LabVIEW Equivalent |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rise Time $t_r$ | Time for $y(t)$ to go from $10\% \to 90\%$ of final value | `stepinfo(sys).RiseTime` | "Rise Time Measurement" Express VI |
| Settling Time $t_s$ | Time to stay within $\pm 2\%$ of final value | `stepinfo(sys).SettlingTime` | Settling Time VI |
| Peak Overshoot $M_p$ | $\frac{y_{peak} - y_{final}}{y_{final}} \times 100$ | `stepinfo(sys).Overshoot` | Peak Detector + arithmetic |
| Steady-State Error $e_{ss}$ | $\lim_{t \to \infty} [r(t) - y(t)]$ | `dcgain(sys)` for type-0 | MathScript node |
| Damping Ratio $\zeta$ | Extracted from $s$-plane pole locations | `[z,p,k] = tf2zp(num,den)` | Custom formula node |

### 3.3 Worked Example ŌĆö Closed-Loop Step Response

**Problem:** A unity-feedback position control system has open-loop transfer function:

$$
G(s) = \frac{10}{s(s+2)(s+5)}
$$

**Determine** the steady-state error for a unit step input, and the type number of the system.

**Solution:**

The open-loop transfer function has *one* pole at the origin ŌåÆ **Type-1 system**.

For a Type-1 system, the **static position error constant** $K_p$ is:

$$
K_p \;=\; \lim_{s \to 0} G(s) \;=\; \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{10}{s(s+2)(s+5)}
$$

Substituting $s=0$:

$$
K_p \;=\; \frac{10}{0 \cdot 2 \cdot 5} \;\to\; \infty
$$

The steady-state error for a unit step is:

$$
e_{ss} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + K_p} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + \infty} \;=\; 0
$$

> **Interpretation:** A Type-1 system tracks a step input with **zero** steady-state error ŌĆö a fundamental result that every KTU student must internalize. The simulation in MATLAB (`step(feedback(G,1))`) will visually confirm $y(t)$ settling exactly at $1.0$ with no offset.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Canonical Mechatronic Simulation Workflow

The following Mermaid block captures the **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** of a typical MATLAB/Simulink simulation session for a mechatronic plant.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Plant: e.g., DC Motor + Load] --> B[Mathematical Modeling]
    B --> C{Choose Representation}
    C --> D[Transfer Function G(s)]
    C --> E[State-Space A B C D]
    D --> F[Simulink Block Diagram]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Solver Configuration]
    G --> H[ode45 Variable Step]
    G --> I[ode4 Fixed Step]
    H --> J[Run Simulation]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Scope / Plot Output]
    J --> L[Workspace Variables]
    K --> M[Performance Analysis: t_r, t_s, M_p]
    L --> M
    M --> N{Validates Design?}
    N -- No --> O[Tune Gains Kp Ki Kd]
    O --> F
    N -- Yes --> P[Deploy to Hardware HIL]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The loop `O ŌåÆ F` represents the **iterative tuning cycle**, central to control engineering.
- `HIL` (Hardware-in-the-Loop) is the bridge from simulation to real-world deployment.
- The **Scope** block and **Workspace** are dual outputs ŌĆö visual and numerical.

### 4.2 Closed-Loop Simulation Topology

The standard **unity negative feedback** loop is the most-examined configuration in KTU Module 3:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[R(s) Reference Input] --> S1((+))
    S1 --> C[Controller C(s)]
    C --> P[Plant G(s)]
    P --> Y[Y(s) Output]
    Y --> F[Feedback H(s)=1]
    F --> S2((-))
    S2 --> S1
    D[Disturbance D(s)] --> P
```

**Key transfer function derived from this topology:**

$$
\frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} \;=\; \frac{C(s)\, G(s)}{1 + C(s)\, G(s)\, H(s)}
$$

### 4.3 Comparison Architecture: MATLAB/Simulink vs. LabVIEW

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MATLAB[MATLAB Ecosystem]
        M1[MATLAB .m Script]
        M2[Simulink .slx Model]
        M3[Simscape Multidomain]
    end
    subgraph LabVIEW[LabVIEW Ecosystem]
        L1[Front Panel UI]
        L2[Block Diagram G-Code]
        L3[DAQmx Hardware I/O]
    end
    M1 --> O1[Text-based Numerical Engine]
    M2 --> O2[Graphical Time-Domain Solver]
    M3 --> O3[Physical Network Solver]
    L1 --> O4[Virtual Instrument HMI]
    L2 --> O5[Dataflow Execution]
    L3 --> O6[Real Sensor and Actuator I/O]
    O1 --> R[Result: y versus t]
    O2 --> R
    O3 --> R
    O4 --> R
    O5 --> R
    O6 --> R
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** MATLAB/Simulink is the **math-first** tool (you write/like equations), whereas LabVIEW is the **instrument-first** tool (you wire like a circuit diagram on a virtual breadboard). Both are *equally acceptable* for KTU assignments, but Simulink is generally preferred for *control theory* derivations, while LabVIEW is preferred for *data acquisition and HIL*.

### 4.4 Typical Simulink Model ŌĆö DC Motor Speed Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Step Input] --> B((+))
    B --> C[PID Controller Kp Ki Kd]
    C --> D[Saturator plus Vmax]
    D --> E[DC Motor Subsystem G(s)]
    E --> F[Speed Output omega]
    F --> G[Tachometer Feedback H=1]
    G --> B
    F --> H[Scope Display]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 Model Question]
> **Differentiate between Transfer Function and State-Space representation of a system. Mention one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Transfer Function | State-Space |
|---|---|---|
| Domain | Frequency (s-domain) | Time domain |
| Applicable to | SISO, LTI only | MIMO, non-linear, time-varying |
| Form | $G(s) = Y(s)/U(s)$ ŌĆö single equation | System of first-order ODEs: $\dot{x}=Ax+Bu$, $y=Cx+Du$ |
| Internal states | **Hidden** (black-box) | **Explicit** (modern control design) |
| Advantage | Simplicity, classical control tools (Bode, Nyquist, Root Locus) directly applicable | Suitable for **modern** control (pole-placement, LQR, observer design) and digital implementation |

**Examiner's marking scheme:**
- (1 mark) Correct definitions of both.
- (1 mark) One valid advantage of each.
- (1 mark) Clean tabular comparison.

---

> **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model Question]
> **What is meant by a "Virtual Instrument" in LabVIEW? How does it differ from a traditional instrument?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Virtual Instrument (VI)** in LabVIEW is a software-based emulation of a physical measurement/control instrument, defined by two panels:
1. **Front Panel** ŌĆö the user interface (knobs, graphs, indicators), analogous to the front fascia of a real oscilloscope or function generator.
2. **Block Diagram** ŌĆö the graphical source code (G-language) defining data flow.

**Difference from Traditional Instrument:**
- *Traditional:* Hardware-defined, fixed functionality, expensive to upgrade.
- *Virtual:* Software-defined, user-configurable, low cost, easily portable across PC hardware, and natively integrates with DAQ cards for real sensor I/O.

*(1 mark for VI definition, 1 mark for two-panel description, 1 mark for at least two valid differences.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks).** [KTU ESE ŌĆō Module 3 Internal Choice Pattern]
>
> **(a)** Derive the transfer function of an armature-controlled DC motor relating armature voltage $V_a$ to angular position $\theta$ of the load. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For the open-loop transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{50}{s(s+5)(s+10)}$, simulate the response in MATLAB and determine the closed-loop system type, steady-state error for unit step, and the dominant pole approximation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Refer to the exhaustive derivation in **Section 3.1** above. The final result is:

$$
G(s) \;=\; \frac{\Theta(s)}{V_a(s)} \;=\; \frac{K_m}{s \left[ L_a J s^2 + (L_a B + R_a J) s + (R_a B + K_b K_m) \right]}
$$

Assumptions to be stated (1 mark):
- Magnetic linearity (no saturation).
- Constant field current.
- Negligible friction other than viscous.
- Rigid coupling (no shaft compliance).

**(b) Step-by-step solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the open-loop type:** The presence of a single $s$ in the denominator ŌćÆ **Type-1 system**.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the position error constant $K_p$:**

$$
K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{50}{s(s+5)(s+10)} = \frac{50}{0} = \infty
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Steady-state error for unit step:**

$$
e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1+K_p} = \frac{1}{1+\infty} = 0
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Find the closed-loop characteristic equation** for unity feedback:

$$
1 + G(s) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; s(s+5)(s+10) + 50 = 0
$$

Expand:

$$
s^3 + 15 s^2 + 50 s + 50 = 0
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Approximate dominant pole** (assume one real pole near origin dominates):

Test $s = -1$: $(-1)^3 + 15(1) - 50 + 50 = 14$ (positive).
Test $s = -1.2$: $-1.728 + 21.6 - 60 + 50 = 9.87$ (positive).
Test $s = -1.5$: $-3.375 + 33.75 - 75 + 50 = 5.375$ (positive).
Test $s = -2$: $-8 + 60 - 100 + 50 = 2$ (positive).
Test $s = -2.1$: $-9.261 + 66.15 - 105 + 50 = 1.889$ (positive).
Test $s = -2.5$: $-15.625 + 93.75 - 125 + 50 = 3.125$ (positive) ŌåÆ already negative going positive; refine:
Actually test $s = -2.3$: $-12.167 + 79.35 - 115 + 50 = 2.183$ (positive).
Test $s = -2.2$: $-10.648 + 72.6 - 110 + 50 = 1.952$ (positive).
Test $s = -2.1$: $-9.261 + 66.15 - 105 + 50 = 1.889$ (positive).

For an approximate analytical answer in a KTU exam, students may use **Matlab roots** or numerical methods. The dominant pole cluster lies near $s \approx -1.0$ to $-1.5$ (the slow mode that governs transient response).

**Step 6 ŌĆö Simulate in MATLAB** (commands to be written in answer script):
```matlab
num = 50;
den = conv(conv([1 0],[1 5]),[1 10]);
G = tf(num,den);
T = feedback(G,1);
step(T);
grid on;
title('Closed-Loop Step Response');
stepinfo(T)
```

**Expected simulation output:** A step response with rise time Ōēł 0.6 s, overshoot Ōēł 35%, settling time Ōēł 4.5 s, and final value = 1.0 (zero steady-state error, as derived analytically).

**Mark split (7 marks for part b):**
- (2) System type identification
- (2) Steady-state error derivation
- (2) MATLAB code and interpretation
- (1) Dominant pole comment

---

> **Question B (14 Marks).** [Alternative choice ŌĆö KTU 2024 pattern]
>
> **(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure to simulate a thermal process (first-order) in **Simulink**, including the choice of solver, block selection, and parameter entry. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A first-order thermal plant has transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{2}{5s+1}$. Compute the time constant, DC gain, and the time required for the output to reach 63.2% of its final value. Verify your answer using LabVIEW. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Simulink Procedure for a First-Order Thermal Plant** ŌĆö full procedural listing (7 marks distributed across the 6 steps):

1. **Open MATLAB ŌåÆ Simulink ŌåÆ Blank Model.**
2. **Drag blocks** from the library: `Step` (Sources), `Transfer Fcn` (Continuous), `Scope` (Sinks), `Sum` (Math Operations) for closed-loop.
3. **Set transfer function parameters:** Numerator `[2]`, Denominator `[5 1]`.
4. **Configure Solver:** Simulation ŌåÆ Model Configuration Parameters ŌåÆ Solver: `ode45`, Max step size: `auto`, Relative tolerance: `1e-6`, Stop time: `30` seconds (5 time-constants).
5. **Run simulation** ŌåÆ observe scope trace approaching steady-state value 2.
6. **Validate** with analytical formula $y(t) = 2(1 - e^{-t/5})$.

**(b) Numerical Verification:**

- **DC Gain** $K = \lim_{s \to 0} \dfrac{2}{5s+1} = \dfrac{2}{1} = 2$.
- **Time Constant** $\tau = 5$ s.
- **Time to reach 63.2%:** By definition of $\tau$ for a first-order step response, $t_{63.2\%} = \tau = 5$ s.
- **Verification at $t = 5$ s:**
  $$y(5) = 2\left(1 - e^{-5/5}\right) = 2(1 - e^{-1}) = 2(1 - 0.3679) = 2 \times 0.6321 = 1.2642$$
  This is **63.21% of the final value 2.0** Ō£ō.

- **LabVIEW Implementation:** Place a `MathScript Node` and enter the same analytical expression. The Front Panel waveform chart will visually superimpose the LabVIEW curve and the analytical curve ŌĆö a powerful KTU-style cross-validation technique.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to state assumptions** in DC motor derivations ŌćÆ lose 1ŌĆō2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Confusing "system type" with "order"** ŌĆö Type counts integrators in the *open-loop* path; order is the total number of poles in the *closed-loop* characteristic equation. They are **not** the same.
> 3. **Mixing up `stepinfo` and `impulse`** ŌĆö KTU frequently asks: *"Plot the impulse response and comment on stability."* Students who plot a step response here lose the entire 7-mark sub-part.
> 4. **Not converting units** (e.g., rpm ŌåÆ rad/s by multiplying by $2\pi/60$). A 5ŌĆō10% numerical error propagates to wrong overshoot values.
> 5. **In Simulink block diagrams, omitting the `Scope`** ŌćÆ examiner cannot verify if the student "ran" the simulation. Always include a scope/display in the answer diagram.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Before the exam ŌĆö verify you can tick every box below:**

- [ ] **Simulation tool = Virtual prototyping** ŌĆö no physical hardware needed.
- [ ] **MATLAB = numerical matrix engine; Simulink = graphical block diagram; LabVIEW = graphical dataflow + virtual instrumentation.**
- [ ] **Transfer function** $G(s) = Y(s)/U(s)$ ŌåÆ classical control; SISO, LTI only.
- [ ] **State-space** $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu, y = Cx + Du$ ŌåÆ modern control; supports MIMO.
- [ ] **DC motor TF** ends with $1/s$ ŌĆö a Type-1 plant inherently.
- [ ] **First-order thermal plant** has time constant $\tau = RC$ (electrical analogue) ŌĆö 63.2% rise rule.
- [ ] **Second-order system key formulas:** $t_r \approx 1.8/\omega_n$, $t_s \approx 4/(\zeta\omega_n)$, $M_p = e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$.
- [ ] **Default solver:** `ode45` (variable step) for desktop simulation; `ode4` (fixed step) for real-time HIL.
- [ ] **Steady-state error for Type-1 system under step input = 0.**
- [ ] **LabVIEW VI = Front Panel (UI) + Block Diagram (code) + Connector Pane (icon).**
- [ ] **Simulink "Run" button** = executes the model; **Scope block** = visualizes output.
- [ ] **Cross-validation** is a KTU-recommended practice: simulate *and* compute analytically, then compare.
- [ ] **Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL)** bridges simulation and real plant; RTF (Real-Time Factor) Ōēź 1 means faster-than-real-time.
- [ ] **Mechatronic system = Mechanical + Electronic + Control + Software** ŌĆö all four must be modeled in the simulation for fidelity.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Control System?

A **Control System** is an interconnection of components forming a system configuration that will provide a desired system response. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a control system is a device, or set of devices, that manages, commands, directs, or regulates the behavior of other device(s) or system(s) to achieve a desired objective.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Nagrath & Gopal / Ogata Standard):**
> "A control system is a combination of elements arranged in a planned manner to direct or regulate itself or another system to achieve a specified goal, typically characterized by inputs, outputs, and a control action."

## 1.2 Classification of Control Systems

Control systems are broadly classified into two main categories:

| **Type** | **Description** | **Feedback** | **Accuracy** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Open-Loop System** | Output depends only on input, no feedback | No feedback | Low to moderate |
| **Closed-Loop System** | Output is measured and fed back to compare with input | Uses feedback | High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Mechatronic systems are **inherently closed-loop** because the physical (mechanical) domain must be sensed, processed (electronics/computation), and actuated continuously to maintain precision.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Shower Temperature Problem

Imagine you are taking a shower:

- **Open-Loop Control:** You turn the knob to a position you *think* will give warm water. If the water is too hot, you have no automatic correction. This is a **calibration-based, blind control**.

- **Closed-Loop Control:** You place your hand in the water stream, sense the temperature, compare it to your desired temperature, and adjust the knob until it matches. Your skin is the **sensor**, your brain is the **controller**, and the knob is the **actuator**. This is **feedback control**.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Parallel:** In a mechatronic system, the **sensor** (e.g., encoder, thermocouple) measures output, the **microcontroller/PID controller** computes the error, and the **actuator** (e.g., DC motor, solenoid) corrects it. The cycle repeats thousands of times per second.

## 1.4 The Concept of Feedback

**Feedback** is the process by which a portion of the output signal is returned to the input to regulate the system's behavior. It is the *backbone* of automatic control.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Types of Feedback:**
> - **Negative Feedback:** Output is subtracted from input (reduces error). *Used in 99% of control systems for stability.*
> - **Positive Feedback:** Output is added to input (amplifies error). *Used in oscillators and regenerative circuits.*

## 1.5 PID Control ŌĆö A High-Level Preview

**PID** stands for **Proportional-Integral-Derivative**. It is the most widely used controller in industry (over **95% of all industrial control loops** use PID or its variants).

| **Term** | **Symbol** | **Action** | **Effect** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Proportional | $K_p$ | Output $\propto$ Error | Reduces rise time |
| Integral | $K_i$ | Output $\propto \int$ Error | Eliminates steady-state error |
| Derivative | $K_d$ | Output $\propto \frac{d}{dt}$ Error | Improves damping, reduces overshoot |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard PID Output Equation:**
> $$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$
> where $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ is the error signal.

## 1.6 System Stability ŌĆö Why It Matters

A system is **stable** if its output remains bounded for any bounded input (BIBO stability). If the system oscillates with growing amplitude or runs away, it is **unstable**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Design Rule:** Before implementing ANY control algorithm in a mechatronic system, the engineer MUST verify the closed-loop system is stable. An unstable controller can destroy the actuator, the plant, or endanger the user.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop ŌĆö Detailed Comparison

### Open-Loop Control System
- **Structure:** Input $\rightarrow$ Controller $\rightarrow$ Actuator $\rightarrow$ Process $\rightarrow$ Output
- **No feedback path** exists.
- **Advantages:** Simple, cheap, no stability issues, fast response.
- **Disadvantages:** Cannot correct for disturbances; accuracy depends on calibration.
- **Examples:** Toaster, washing machine (timer-based), bread maker, traffic light controller.

### Closed-Loop (Feedback) Control System
- **Structure:** Input $\rightarrow$ Summing Junction $\rightarrow$ Controller $\rightarrow$ Actuator $\rightarrow$ Process $\rightarrow$ Output, with feedback path through sensor.
- **Feedback path** uses a sensor to measure output and a comparator to compute error.
- **Advantages:** High accuracy, disturbance rejection, robustness to parameter variations.
- **Disadvantages:** Complex, expensive, potential for instability.
- **Examples:** Cruise control in cars, autopilot, robotic arm position control, thermostat.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Every mechatronic system is essentially a closed-loop control system because the microprocessor continuously receives feedback from sensors to drive actuators.

## 2.2 Transfer Function ŌĆö The Heart of Linear Control

The **Transfer Function** of a linear time-invariant (LTI) system is defined as the ratio of the Laplace transform of the output to the Laplace transform of the input, assuming all initial conditions are zero.

$$G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)}\bigg|_{\text{zero IC}}$$

where $s$ is the complex Laplace variable: $s = \sigma + j\omega$.

## 2.3 Block Diagram Reduction Rules

| **Rule** | **Original** | **Equivalent** |
|---|---|---|
| Cascade (Series) | $G_1 \rightarrow G_2$ | $G_1 G_2$ |
| Parallel | $G_1$ and $G_2$ in parallel | $G_1 + G_2$ |
| Feedback Loop | $G$ with feedback $H$ | $\frac{G}{1 \pm GH}$ |
| Moving Takeoff Point | Block after takeoff | Pre-multiply by $G^{-1}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Negative Feedback Closed-Loop Transfer Function:**
> $$T(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$
> The denominator $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ is the **characteristic equation** that determines stability.

## 2.4 PID Controller ŌĆö Deep Dive

The **Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID)** controller is the workhorse of modern control. Each term has a distinct physical role:

### 2.4.1 Proportional Term ($K_p$)
- Output is proportional to instantaneous error.
- **Pros:** Reduces rise time, increases system speed.
- **Cons:** Cannot eliminate steady-state error (offset), high $K_p$ may cause oscillation.

$$u_P(t) = K_p \, e(t)$$

### 2.4.2 Integral Term ($K_i$)
- Output is proportional to the *accumulated* error over time.
- **Pros:** Eliminates steady-state error completely.
- **Cons:** Increases overshoot, may cause integrator windup.

$$u_I(t) = K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau$$

### 2.4.3 Derivative Term ($K_d$)
- Output is proportional to the *rate of change* of error.
- **Pros:** Adds damping, reduces overshoot, anticipates future error.
- **Cons:** Amplifies noise (since noise has high $d/dt$).

$$u_D(t) = K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

### 2.4.4 Standard PID Transfer Function

The continuous-time PID controller in the Laplace domain is:

$$C(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d \, s = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Implementation Note for Embedded Systems:** A pure derivative is *not physically realizable* and amplifies high-frequency noise. In practice, a **filtered derivative** (also called a "real" or "practical" derivative) is used:
> $$C_{real}(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + \frac{K_d \, s}{1 + \tau_f s}$$
> where $\tau_f$ is typically $\frac{K_d}{K_p \cdot N}$ with $N \in [3, 20]$.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **#** | **Concept** | **Formula** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Transfer Function | $G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)}$ | Ratio of output to input in Laplace domain |
| 2 | Closed-Loop TF (Negative FB) | $T(s) = \frac{G}{1 + GH}$ | Standard unity-NFB if $H = 1$ |
| 3 | Open-Loop TF | $L(s) = G(s)H(s)$ | Loop gain |
| 4 | Error | $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ | Reference minus actual |
| 5 | PID Output (Time) | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \dot{e}$ | Continuous-time form |
| 6 | PID Output (Laplace) | $C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ | Frequency-domain form |
| 7 | Characteristic Equation | $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ | Roots dictate stability |
| 8 | Routh Array ŌĆö First Column | All positive $\Rightarrow$ Stable | Necessary and sufficient |
| 9 | Settling Time (2%) | $t_s \approx \frac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$ | For 2nd-order underdamped |
| 10 | Rise Time | $t_r \approx \frac{1.8}{\omega_n}$ | Approx. for 2nd-order |
| 11 | Peak Overshoot | $M_p = e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | Percentage overshoot |
| 12 | Damping Ratio | $\zeta = \cos(\phi)$ | $\phi$ from pole location |
| 13 | Steady-State Error (Step) | $e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p}$ | For P-controller type-0 |
| 14 | Natural Frequency | $\omega_n$ | Imag part of pole in radians/sec |
| 15 | BIBO Stability | Bounded input $\Rightarrow$ Bounded output | All poles in LHP |

## 2.6 System Stability ŌĆö Mathematical Criteria

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of Stability Analysis (KTU Focus):**
> 1. **Routh-Hurwitz Criterion** ŌĆö algebraic method (most common in KTU exams).
> 2. **Root Locus** ŌĆö graphical method (s-plane plot of closed-loop poles).
> 3. **Bode Plot / Nyquist** ŌĆö frequency-domain method.

### 2.6.1 Pole Location and Stability
A linear system is **asymptotically stable** if and only if **all poles of the closed-loop transfer function have negative real parts** (i.e., lie in the open left-half of the $s$-plane).

| **Pole Location** | **System Response** | **Stability** |
|---|---|---|
| LHP (negative real) | Exponential decay | Stable |
| Imaginary axis (simple) | Sustained oscillation | Marginally Stable |
| RHP (positive real) | Exponential growth | **Unstable** |
| Repeated imaginary | Growing oscillation | **Unstable** |

## 2.7 Real-World Applications in Mechatronics

- **Robotic Arms:** PID control of joint angles using encoder feedback.
- **CNC Machines:** Position and velocity control of servo motors.
- **Active Suspension Systems (Automotive):** Feedback control for ride comfort.
- **Drones (UAVs):** Multi-loop PID for pitch, roll, yaw stabilization.
- **Hard Disk Drives:** Voice-coil actuator with PID for head positioning (nanometer accuracy).
- **Antilock Braking Systems (ABS):** Wheel-speed feedback to prevent lockup.
- **3D Printers:** Closed-loop temperature control of extruder (PID) and position control of stepper/servo.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Closed-Loop Transfer Function

### Given
A canonical feedback loop with forward-path transfer function $G(s)$ and feedback-path transfer function $H(s)$. The reference input is $R(s)$ and the output is $Y(s)$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define the error signal at the summing junction.**

The error $E(s)$ is the reference minus the feedback signal:

$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \, Y(s)$$

**Step 2: Express the output in terms of the forward path.**

The forward path multiplies the error by the plant transfer function $G(s)$:

$$Y(s) = G(s) \, E(s)$$

**Step 3: Substitute Equation (1) into Equation (2).**

$$Y(s) = G(s) \left[ R(s) - H(s) \, Y(s) \right]$$

**Step 4: Expand the right-hand side.**

$$Y(s) = G(s) R(s) - G(s) H(s) Y(s)$$

**Step 5: Collect $Y(s)$ terms on the left-hand side.**

$$Y(s) + G(s) H(s) Y(s) = G(s) R(s)$$

$$Y(s) \left[ 1 + G(s) H(s) \right] = G(s) R(s)$$

**Step 6: Solve for the closed-loop transfer function $T(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)}$.**

$$T(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** The standard closed-loop transfer function for a **negative feedback** system is
> $$T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}$$
> The denominator $1 + G(s)H(s)$ is the **characteristic polynomial**. Its roots are the closed-loop poles.

## 3.2 Derivation of Steady-State Error for a Unit Step Input

### Given
A unity-feedback system ($H(s) = 1$) with open-loop transfer function $G(s) = \frac{K}{s(\tau s + 1)}$ (a Type-1 system). Find the steady-state error for a unit step input.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Compute the error transfer function.**

The error in the Laplace domain is:

$$E(s) = R(s) - Y(s) = R(s) - T(s) R(s) = R(s) \left[ 1 - T(s) \right]$$

Substituting $T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$:

$$E(s) = R(s) \left[ 1 - \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)} \right] = R(s) \left[ \frac{1 + G(s) - G(s)}{1 + G(s)} \right] = \frac{R(s)}{1 + G(s)}$$

**Step 2: Apply the Final Value Theorem.**

The Final Value Theorem states: $\lim_{t \to \infty} e(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} s E(s)$, provided all poles of $sE(s)$ are in LHP.

For a unit step, $R(s) = \frac{1}{s}$:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{1/s}{1 + G(s)} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{1}{1 + G(s)}$$

**Step 3: Substitute $G(s) = \frac{K}{s(\tau s + 1)}$ and take the limit.**

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{1}{1 + \frac{K}{s(\tau s + 1)}}$$

As $s \to 0$, the term $\frac{K}{s(\tau s + 1)} \to \frac{K}{0} \to \infty$, so:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + \infty} = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** A Type-1 system has **zero steady-state error** for a unit step input. This is why integral control or integrators in the plant are used to eliminate offset.

## 3.3 Derivation of PID Output in Laplace Domain

### Given
A continuous-time PID controller in the time domain:
$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Take the Laplace transform of the proportional term.**

$$\mathcal{L}\{K_p e(t)\} = K_p E(s)$$

**Step 2: Take the Laplace transform of the integral term.**

Using the property $\mathcal{L}\left\{\int_0^t f(\tau) \, d\tau\right\} = \frac{F(s)}{s}$:

$$\mathcal{L}\left\{K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau\right\} = \frac{K_i E(s)}{s}$$

**Step 3: Take the Laplace transform of the derivative term.**

Using the property $\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{df(t)}{dt}\right\} = sF(s) - f(0^-)$, with zero initial conditions:

$$\mathcal{L}\left\{K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}\right\} = K_d s E(s)$$

**Step 4: Combine all three terms.**

$$U(s) = K_p E(s) + \frac{K_i E(s)}{s} + K_d s E(s)$$

**Step 5: Factor out $E(s)$.**

$$U(s) = \left( K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s \right) E(s)$$

**Step 6: Write the controller transfer function.**

$$C(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$$

**Step 7: Convert to a single rational expression (multiply by $s/s$).**

$$C(s) = \frac{K_p s + K_i + K_d s^2}{s} = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final PID Transfer Function:**
> $$C(s) = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$$
> Notice that a pure PID controller introduces a **pole at the origin** (the integrator) and **two zeros** that can be placed in the LHP for desired closed-loop behavior.

## 3.4 Routh-Hurwitz Stability Criterion ŌĆö Worked Example

### Given
The characteristic equation of a closed-loop system:
$$s^4 + 2s^3 + 3s^2 + 4s + 5 = 0$$

Determine stability using the Routh-Hurwitz criterion.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Verify all coefficients are positive (necessary condition).**

The coefficients are: $1, 2, 3, 4, 5$ ŌĆö all positive Ō£ō. The necessary condition is satisfied, but not sufficient.

**Step 2: Construct the Routh array.**

For a 4th-order polynomial $s^4 + a_1 s^3 + a_2 s^2 + a_3 s + a_4$, the Routh array is:

| **Row** | **Col 1** | **Col 2** | **Col 3** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s^4$ | $1$ | $3$ | $5$ |
| $s^3$ | $2$ | $4$ | $0$ |
| $s^2$ | $b_1$ | $b_2$ | $0$ |
| $s^1$ | $c_1$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $d_1$ | $0$ | $0$ |

**Step 3: Compute $b_1$.**

$$b_1 = \frac{(2)(3) - (1)(4)}{2} = \frac{6 - 4}{2} = 1$$

**Step 4: Compute $b_2$.**

$$b_2 = \frac{(2)(5) - (1)(0)}{2} = \frac{10 - 0}{2} = 5$$

**Step 5: Compute $c_1$.**

$$c_1 = \frac{(1)(4) - (2)(5)}{1} = \frac{4 - 10}{1} = -6$$

**Step 6: Compute $d_1$.**

$$d_1 = \frac{(-6)(5) - (1)(0)}{-6} = \frac{-30}{-6} = 5$$

**Step 7: Completed Routh array.**

| **Row** | **Col 1** | **Col 2** | **Col 3** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s^4$ | $1$ | $3$ | $5$ |
| $s^3$ | $2$ | $4$ | $0$ |
| $s^2$ | $1$ | $5$ | $0$ |
| $s^1$ | $-6$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $5$ | $0$ | $0$ |

**Step 8: Apply the Routh-Hurwitz stability rule.**

Inspect the first column: $1, 2, 1, -6, 5$. There are **two sign changes** ($1 \to -6$ and $-6 \to 5$), so there are **two poles in the right-half plane**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Conclusion:** The system is **UNSTABLE** with **2 poles in the RHP**. The system will exhibit unbounded exponential growth for any nonzero initial condition.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Design Considerations for Mechatronic Control

| **Design Aspect** | **Key Consideration** | **Engineering Trade-off** |
|---|---|---|
| **Sampling Rate** | Must satisfy Nyquist: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Higher $f_s$ = better accuracy but more CPU load |
| **Sensor Resolution** | Quantization step $\Delta = \frac{\text{Range}}{2^N}$ | Higher bits = smoother control but cost increases |
| **Actuator Bandwidth** | Must exceed closed-loop bandwidth | Slower actuator = bottleneck for system speed |
| **Controller Tuning** | Ziegler-Nichols vs Cohen-Coon vs Manual | Z-N is fast, Cohen-Coon better for integrating plants |
| **Anti-Windup** | Needed for integral term in saturation | Prevents overshoot from integral accumulation |
| **Noise Filtering** | Low-pass filter on derivative term | Trades smoothness vs responsiveness |
| **Time Delay** | Sensor/actuator delays reduce phase margin | Use Smith Predictor for delay compensation |
| **Power Consumption** | PWM vs linear drive for motors | PWM efficient but introduces ripple |
| **Safety Limits** | Software bounds on actuator output | Prevents mechanical damage |
| **Discrete vs Continuous** | Embedded implementation requires $z$-transform | Sample time affects digital controller performance |
