# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define a control system. Distinguish between open-loop and closed-loop control systems with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **control system** is a combination of physical components arranged in a structured manner to command, direct, or regulate itself or another system to achieve a desired output. The reference and feedback are the two cardinal elements in any control system.

**Open-Loop Control System:** An open-loop system is one in which the control action is independent of the output. There is no feedback path. The accuracy of the output depends entirely on the calibration of the input. **Example:** A washing machine that operates on a preset timer without sensing the dirt level or weight of clothes. If the load changes, the washing quality degrades.

**Closed-Loop Control System:** A closed-loop (or feedback) system is one in which the control action depends on the output. A portion of the output is measured by a sensor, fed back to a summing junction, and compared with the reference input to generate an error signal. **Example:** A household refrigerator that senses the internal temperature using a thermostat and switches the compressor on or off to maintain the set temperature.

**Key Differences:**

| **Feature** | **Open-Loop** | **Closed-Loop** |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback | Absent | Present |
| Accuracy | Low | High |
| Disturbance Rejection | Poor | Excellent |
| Stability | Always stable | Requires analysis |
| Cost & Complexity | Low | Higher |
| Bandwidth | Wide | Limited by loop |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners expect **two valid examples** and at least **three distinguishing points** for full marks. Mentioning the feedback path is essential.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**Explain the terms rise time, settling time, peak overshoot, and steady-state error as applied to the step response of a control system.**

**Model Answer:**

These four metrics are the **time-domain performance indices** used to evaluate a control system's transient and steady-state behavior when subjected to a unit step input.

**1. Rise Time ($t_r$):** It is the time required for the response to rise from **0% to 100%** of its final value (or 10% to 90% in some definitions) for an underdamped second-order system. It is inversely proportional to the natural frequency. Mathematically, for a standard second-order system: $t_r \approx \frac{1.8}{\omega_n}$ where $\omega_n$ is the natural frequency in rad/s.

**2. Peak Overshoot ($M_p$):** It is the maximum peak value of the response curve measured from the steady-state (final) value, expressed as a percentage. It occurs due to underdamping and is mathematically given by:
$$M_p = e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \times 100\%$$
where $\zeta$ is the damping ratio. A higher $K_p$ in a PID controller typically *increases* overshoot.

**3. Settling Time ($t_s$):** It is the time required for the response to reach and stay within a specified tolerance band (commonly $\pm 2\%$ or $\pm 5\%$) of the final value. For a 2% criterion:
$$t_s \approx \frac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$$
Settling time is dominated by the **dominant real pole** in the s-plane.

**4. Steady-State Error ($e_{ss}$):** It is the difference between the desired output (reference) and the actual output as $t \to \infty$. For a unit step input in a unity-feedback system:
$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p}$$
for a P-controller. The integral term in a PID controller is specifically designed to **drive $e_{ss}$ to zero** for step inputs.

> [!TIP]
> **Design Trade-off:** A faster rise time (higher $K_p$) usually increases overshoot and settling time. A well-tuned controller balances all four indices.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**
Derive the closed-loop transfer function of a negative feedback system with forward path $G(s)$ and feedback path $H(s)$. Show that the characteristic equation of the system is $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Define the system variables.**
Let $R(s)$ be the reference input, $Y(s)$ the output, $E(s)$ the error signal at the summing junction, and $G(s)$ and $H(s)$ the forward and feedback transfer functions respectively.

**Step 2: Express the error.**
The error at the summing junction for a negative feedback configuration is the reference minus the feedback signal:
$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \, Y(s) \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Step 3: Express the output through the forward path.**
The output is the product of the error and the forward path transfer function:
$$Y(s) = G(s) \, E(s) \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 4: Substitute equation (1) into equation (2).**
$$Y(s) = G(s) \left[ R(s) - H(s) \, Y(s) \right]$$

**Step 5: Expand and collect $Y(s)$ terms on the LHS.**
$$Y(s) = G(s) R(s) - G(s) H(s) Y(s)$$
$$Y(s) + G(s) H(s) Y(s) = G(s) R(s)$$
$$Y(s) \left[ 1 + G(s) H(s) \right] = G(s) R(s)$$

**Step 6: Write the closed-loop transfer function.**
$$T(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}$$

**Step 7: Identify the characteristic equation.**
The denominator $1 + G(s)H(s)$ determines the dynamic behavior. The **characteristic equation** is obtained by setting the denominator to zero:
$$1 + G(s) H(s) = 0$$
The roots of this polynomial are the **closed-loop poles** of the system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** For the closed-loop system to be stable, **all roots of $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$** must lie strictly in the left half of the $s$-plane.

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**
For the system with $G(s) = \frac{10}{s(s+2)(s+4)}$ and $H(s) = 1$, determine the range of gain $K$ for which the closed-loop system is stable, using the Routh-Hurwitz criterion. Assume an additional gain $K$ is placed in the forward path.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Write the open-loop transfer function with the gain $K$.**
$$G_{aug}(s) = \frac{10K}{s(s+2)(s+4)}, \quad H(s) = 1$$

**Step 2: Form the characteristic equation $1 + G_{aug}(s) H(s) = 0$.**
$$1 + \frac{10K}{s(s+2)(s+4)} = 0$$
$$s(s+2)(s+4) + 10K = 0$$

**Step 3: Expand $s(s+2)(s+4)$.**
$$s(s+2)(s+4) = s(s^2 + 6s + 8) = s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s$$
So the characteristic equation becomes:
$$s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s + 10K = 0$$

**Step 4: Construct the Routh array for $s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s + 10K = 0$.**

| **Row** | **Col 1** | **Col 2** |
|---|---|---|
| $s^3$ | $1$ | $8$ |
| $s^2$ | $6$ | $10K$ |
| $s^1$ | $b_1$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $c_1$ | $0$ |

**Step 5: Compute $b_1$.**
$$b_1 = \frac{(6)(8) - (1)(10K)}{6} = \frac{48 - 10K}{6} = \frac{24 - 5K}{3}$$

**Step 6: Compute $c_1$.**
$$c_1 = \frac{b_1 \cdot 10K - 6 \cdot 0}{b_1} = 10K$$

**Step 7: Apply the stability condition.**
For stability, the first column must have **all positive** entries:
- Row $s^3$: $1 > 0$ Ō£ō (always)
- Row $s^2$: $6 > 0$ Ō£ō (always)
- Row $s^1$: $b_1 > 0 \Rightarrow \frac{24 - 5K}{3} > 0 \Rightarrow K < \frac{24}{5} = 4.8$
- Row $s^0$: $c_1 > 0 \Rightarrow 10K > 0 \Rightarrow K > 0$

**Step 8: Combine the inequalities.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Range of Stability:** $\boxed{0 < K < 4.8}$
> At $K = 4.8$, the system is **marginally stable** (sustained oscillation at $\omega = \sqrt{8} = 2\sqrt{2}$ rad/s). For $K > 4.8$, the system is **unstable**.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**
With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a PID controller. Derive the expression for the control output in the Laplace domain.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Block Diagram Description.**

A PID controller continuously computes an error value $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ and applies a correction based on three terms: Proportional, Integral, and Derivative. The block diagram consists of three parallel paths whose outputs are summed.

**Step 2: Time-Domain Expression.**

The PID output in the time domain is:
$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3: Apply Laplace Transform to Each Term.**

Using the linearity of the Laplace transform:

- Proportional term: $\mathcal{L}\{K_p e(t)\} = K_p E(s)$
- Integral term: $\mathcal{L}\left\{K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau\right\} = \frac{K_i}{s} E(s)$
- Derivative term: $\mathcal{L}\left\{K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}\right\} = K_d s E(s)$ (assuming zero initial conditions)

**Step 4: Combine All Three Terms in the s-Domain.**

$$U(s) = K_p E(s) + \frac{K_i}{s} E(s) + K_d s E(s) = \left( K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s \right) E(s)$$

**Step 5: Form the Controller Transfer Function.**

$$C(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$$

**Step 6: Express as a Single Rational Function.**

Multiply numerator and denominator by $s$:
$$C(s) = \frac{K_p s + K_i + K_d s^2}{s} = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$$

**Step 7: Physical Interpretation of Each Term.**

| **Term** | **Effect on System** | **Effect on Stability** |
|---|---|---|
| $K_p$ | Reduces rise time | Can cause oscillation if too high |
| $K_i$ | Eliminates steady-state error | Adds phase lag (slower response) |
| $K_d$ | Increases damping, reduces overshoot | Sensitive to noise |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The PID controller has **one pole at the origin** (integrator) and **two zeros** whose locations depend on $K_p, K_i, K_d$. The zeros can be placed to shape the closed-loop response.

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**
Explain the design considerations that must be addressed when implementing a closed-loop controller in a mechatronic system. Discuss sampling rate, anti-windup, and sensor noise filtering.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Sampling Rate Selection:**
- The digital controller samples the error at discrete time intervals $T_s$. According to the **Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem**, the sampling frequency $f_s$ must be at least twice the highest frequency component of interest: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.
- In practice, $f_s$ is chosen to be **10 to 20 times** the desired closed-loop bandwidth to allow for anti-aliasing filtering and discrete-time stability margins.
- **Trade-off:** Higher $f_s$ improves performance but increases computational load on the microcontroller/DSP.

**2. Anti-Windup Protection:**
- When the actuator reaches its physical saturation limit (e.g., motor max voltage, valve fully open), the integral term continues to accumulate error, a phenomenon called **integrator windup**.
- This causes large overshoot and poor recovery when the setpoint is finally achievable.
- **Solution:** Implement **conditional integration** (clamping) or use the **back-calculation method**, where the difference between the saturated and unsaturated control output is fed back to the integrator input with gain $K_t$.

**3. Sensor Noise Filtering:**
- Real sensors produce noisy measurements (e.g., encoder quantization, electrical interference on thermocouples).
- The **derivative term** $K_d \frac{de}{dt}$ amplifies high-frequency noise, making the controller "twitchy" or even unstable.
- **Solution:** Use a **low-pass filter** on the measured output or on the derivative term alone. The practical derivative becomes:
$$C_{real}(s) = K_d \frac{s}{1 + \tau_f s}$$
where $\tau_f$ is a small filter time constant (typically $K_d / (K_p N)$ with $N \in [3, 20]$).

**4. Additional Critical Considerations:**

| **Consideration** | **Approach** |
|---|---|
| **Plant Uncertainty** | Robust control with gain/phase margins $\geq 6$ dB and $\geq 45┬░$ |
| **Actuator Saturation** | Software limits on $u(t)$ with anti-windup |
| **Time Delays** | Smith Predictor or model-predictive compensation |
| **Discretization** | Use Tustin (bilinear) transform for digital implementation |
| **Power Supply** | Decoupling capacitors, EMI shielding on motor cables |
| **Safety** | Hardware emergency stop + software watchdog timers |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT skip the Laplace derivation steps.** Examiners allocate **3 of 7 marks** in Part (a) to the step-by-step Laplace transform of each PID term. Jumping directly to the final answer loses those marks.
> - **For Part (b),** simply listing terms without explaining the *trade-off* (e.g., "high sampling = good but CPU-heavy") will cap your marks. Always state **WHY** a design choice is made.
> - **Common mistake:** Writing $H(s) = 0$ instead of $H(s) = 1$ for unity feedback. Check the problem statement carefully.
> - **Stability problems:** Forgetting to include $K$ in the characteristic equation is a frequent error. Substitute the open-loop TF *with gain* into $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ before applying Routh.

- **Open-loop:** no feedback, simple, inaccurate. **Closed-loop:** feedback, accurate, may be unstable.
- **Transfer function** $G(s) = Y(s)/R(s)$ with zero initial conditions.
- **Closed-loop TF** (negative feedback): $T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$.
- **Characteristic equation:** $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ governs closed-loop poles and hence stability.
- **Stability condition:** All closed-loop poles must have **strictly negative real parts** (LHP).
- **Routh-Hurwitz:** Build array; if all first-column entries are positive, system is stable. Number of sign changes = number of RHP poles.
- **PID Output (time):** $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \dot{e}$.
- **PID Transfer Function (s-domain):** $C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$.
- **$K_p$:** reduces rise time, can cause overshoot. **$K_i$:** eliminates steady-state error. **$K_d$:** adds damping, sensitive to noise.
- **Type-0 system** with P-control: nonzero $e_{ss}$ for step. **Type-1 or higher** with any controller: zero $e_{ss}$ for step.
- **BIBO stability:** Bounded input $\Rightarrow$ Bounded output. All poles in LHP.
- **Sampling theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist rate); in practice $f_s \approx 10\times$ bandwidth.
- **Anti-windup** is mandatory when actuator saturates to prevent integrator runaway.
- **Practical PID uses filtered derivative** to suppress high-frequency noise.
- **Mechatronic design hierarchy:** outer position loop $\rightarrow$ middle velocity loop $\rightarrow$ inner current loop (each 10x faster than the outer).
- **Ziegler-Nichols** tuning method: Step 1 = find $K_u$ (ultimate gain) and $P_u$ (ultimate period). Step 2 = set $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = 0.5 P_u$, $T_d = 0.125 P_u$.
- **Settling time** $t_s \approx \frac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$ (2% criterion). **Overshoot** $M_p = e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$.
- Always state **assumptions** (linearity, time-invariance, zero initial conditions) before deriving a transfer function.
- **Block diagram reduction:** series $\rightarrow$ multiply, parallel $\rightarrow$ add, feedback $\rightarrow \frac{G}{1 \pm GH}$.
- **Discrete implementation:** use Tustin transform: $s = \frac{2}{T_s} \frac{z-1}{z+1}$ for digital PID.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:** In stability problems, **always form the characteristic equation first**, **then** apply Routh. Do not try to evaluate $G(s)$ at $s = 0$ unless specifically asked for steady-state error.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implementation and Testing: Practical Implementation of Control Algorithms on Hardware Platforms; Testing and Validation of Mechatronic Systems Using Simulation and Real-World Experiments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT861 - MECHATRONIC SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Implementation and Testing: Practical Implementation of Control Algorithms on Hardware Platforms; Testing and Validation of Mechatronic Systems Using Simulation and Real-World Experiments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Implementation of Control Algorithms on Hardware

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Practical Implementation of Control Algorithms on Hardware Platforms* refers to the process of translating a mathematically formulated control law (PID, state-space, fuzzy, adaptive, etc.) into executable code that runs on a physical computational platform ŌĆö such as a microcontroller, Digital Signal Processor (DSP), Field-Programmable Gate Array (FPGA), or Programmable Logic Controller (PLC) ŌĆö to drive real actuators and read real sensors in a mechatronic system.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a control algorithm as a **recipe**, the hardware platform as the **kitchen**, and the physical mechatronic system as the **dish being cooked**. A beautifully written recipe (control law) is useless until a chef (hardware) actually follows it in a real kitchen. The "chef" must:

1. Read the ingredients in real time (sensor sampling)
2. Decide how much spice to add (compute control action)
3. Stir the pot within milliseconds (actuator command)
4. Repeat thousands of times per minute (real-time loop)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Realisation:** Hardware implementation is **not** the same as simulation. The physical world introduces **delays, quantization, noise, saturation, and non-linearities** that pure mathematical models ignore.

### Standard Physical Constants / Metrics in Implementation

- **Sampling Frequency ($f_s$):** Typically **1 kHz to 100 kHz** for motion control; **1ŌĆō10 kHz** for process control
- **ADC Resolution:** **8-bit, 10-bit, 12-bit, 16-bit**
- **Control Loop Latency Target:** **< 1 ms** for high-performance servos
- **Clock Frequency ($f_{clk}$):** **16 MHz ŌĆō 1 GHz** depending on platform

---

## 1.2 Testing and Validation of Mechatronic Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Testing and Validation* is the systematic process of confirming that a mechatronic system meets its specified functional, performance, and safety requirements under both simulated and real operating conditions. It is the bridge between *design confidence* and *deployment readiness*.

### Conceptual Analogy: The Airplane Crash-Test Analogy

Before a new aircraft flies passengers, engineers run:
- **Computer crash simulations** (MIL / SIL) ŌĆö like playing a flight sim
- **Cockpit rehearsals in a fake cockpit** (HIL) ŌĆö like a pilot in a stationary simulator
- **Real test flights** (Real-World Experiments) ŌĆö actual takeoff, real risk

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification vs Validation (V&V):**
> - **Verification** = "Are we building the system **right**?" (Does the code match the design?)
> - **Validation** = "Are we building the **right** system?" (Does it solve the customer's problem?)

### Three Pillars of Mechatronic Testing

| Pillar | Environment | Risk | Cost | Fidelity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Simulation (MIL/SIL)** | Pure software | Very Low | Low | Medium |
| **Hardware-in-Loop (HIL)** | Hardware + simulated plant | Medium | Medium | High |
| **Real-World Experiments** | Real plant + real hardware | High | High | Highest |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Pearl:** The testing hierarchy follows a *left-to-right increasing fidelity* model. Always start testing at the cheapest, safest level and progress rightward only when prior tests pass.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hardware Implementation: The Operational Pipeline

The transition from a designed control law to a working mechatronic system follows a **strict 6-step pipeline**:

1. **Plant Modelling & Control Design (Off-line):**
   Mathematical model $G(s)$ or state-space $(A,B,C,D)$ is derived, and a compensator $C(s)$ is designed in MATLAB/Simulink.

2. **Discretisation (Continuous ŌåÆ Discrete):**
   The continuous compensator $C(s)$ is converted to a difference equation $C(z)$ using:
   - **Forward Euler:** $s \approx \dfrac{z-1}{T_s}$
   - **Backward Euler:** $s \approx \dfrac{z-1}{zT_s}$
   - **Tustin's (Bilinear) Method:** $s \approx \dfrac{2}{T_s}\dfrac{z-1}{z+1}$

3. **Code Generation & Cross-Compilation:**
   Auto-generated C code (Simulink Coder, Embedded Coder) or hand-written C is compiled for the target processor.

4. **Real-Time Scheduling:**
   The control task is bound to a deterministic timer interrupt with strict deadline guarantees.

5. **I/O Driver Configuration:**
   ADC, PWM, quadrature encoder, and communication peripherals are configured to interface with sensors and actuators.

6. **Deployment & Commissioning:**
   Code is flashed to the controller; tuning of gains happens in situ with the live plant.

---

## 2.2 Hardware Platform Selection Matrix

| Platform | Typical Clock | Strengths | Weaknesses | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Microcontroller (MCU)** ŌĆö Arduino, STM32, PIC | 16ŌĆō500 MHz | Low cost, low power, rich peripherals | Single-core, no parallel compute | Small embedded controllers, education |
| **DSP** ŌĆö TMS320F28379D | 100ŌĆō300 MHz | Fast MAC units, fixed-point math | Steep learning curve | Motor control, audio, signal processing |
| **FPGA** ŌĆö Xilinx Zynq, Intel Cyclone | 50ŌĆō500 MHz logic rate | True parallelism, deterministic latency | Expensive, HDL required | High-speed control, vision, hardware-accelerated loops |
| **PLC** ŌĆö Siemens S7, Allen-Bradley | Scan time 1ŌĆō20 ms | Industrial ruggedness, safety certs | Slow loops, proprietary | Factory automation, process control |
| **Industrial PC + RTOS** | 1ŌĆō4 GHz | High-level languages, complex algorithms | Cost, jitter without real-time kernel | Robotics, autonomous systems, research |

---

## 2.3 Critical Implementation Issues (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5 Implementation Killers:**

1. **Aliasing** ŌĆö When $f_s < 2 f_{max}$ of the plant, false frequencies appear. Always apply an **anti-aliasing filter** before sampling.
2. **Quantisation** ŌĆö ADC resolution of $n$ bits gives a step size $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$.
3. **Computational Delay** ŌĆö If the algorithm takes $T_c$ to compute, the effective loop is delayed.
4. **Integer Overflow / Windup** ŌĆö Accumulator registers wrap around; integrators in PID saturate.
5. **Jitter** ŌĆö Variable time between samples reduces phase margin. Determinism matters more than raw speed.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Equation / Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $f_s$ | Sampling frequency | $f_s = \dfrac{1}{T_s}$ | Hz |
| $T_s$ | Sampling period | $T_s = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$ | s |
| Nyquist Rate | Minimum safe sampling | $f_s > 2 f_{max}$ | Hz |
| ADC step size | Quantisation | $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | V |
| SNR (ADC) | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $\mathrm{SNR} = 6.02n + 1.76$ | dB |
| Forward Euler | $s \to z$ | $s \approx \dfrac{z-1}{T_s}$ | ŌĆö |
| Backward Euler | $s \to z$ | $s \approx \dfrac{z-1}{zT_s}$ | ŌĆö |
| Tustin (Bilinear) | $s \to z$ | $s \approx \dfrac{2}{T_s}\dfrac{z-1}{z+1}$ | ŌĆö |
| Phase lag from ZOH | Zero-Order-Hold | $\phi_{ZOH} \approx -\omega \dfrac{T_s}{2}$ | rad |
| Discrete PID | Position form | $u[k] = K_p e[k] + K_i \sum e[k]T_s + K_d \dfrac{e[k]-e[k-1]}{T_s}$ | ŌĆö |
| Discrete PID | Velocity form | $\Delta u[k] = K_p (e[k]-e[k-1]) + K_i e[k]T_s + K_d \dfrac{e[k]-2e[k-1]+e[k-2]}{T_s}$ | ŌĆö |
| Stability (Jury) | Schur-Cohn condition for $D(z)=a_0 z^2 + a_1 z + a_2$ | $\vert a_2 \vert < a_0$ and $a_0+a_1+a_2 > 0$ | ŌĆö |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Safeguard:** All absolute value bars $\vert \cdot \vert$ above use `\vert` so the markdown table does not break. KTU scripts expect symbols **NOT** to clash with table column delimiters.

---

## 2.5 Testing & Validation Frameworks

### V-Model of Mechatronic System Development

The V-Model pairs each design phase (descending left leg) with a corresponding test phase (ascending right leg):

- **System Requirements** Ōåö **Acceptance Testing** (real customer scenario)
- **Subsystem Design** Ōåö **Integration Testing** (subsystems working together)
- **Component Design** Ōåö **Unit Testing** (PID tuning, sensor calibration)
- **Coding** Ōåö **Static Analysis** (lint, MISRA-C compliance)

### Loop-Level Simulation Categories

| Loop | Acronym | What's Real? | What's Simulated? | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Model-in-the-Loop** | MIL | Nothing | Plant + Controller (both in software) | Early algorithm validation |
| **Software-in-the-Loop** | SIL | Nothing | Plant (model) + Controller (compiled code on PC) | Verify generated code behaviour |
| **Processor-in-the-Loop** | PIL | Real target processor | Plant (model on PC) | Verify cross-compiled code timing/IO |
| **Hardware-in-the-Loop** | HIL | Real controller (or ECU) | Plant (real-time simulator) | Stress-test controller against emulated plant |
| **Real-World Test** | ŌĆö | Everything | Nothing | Final qualification |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Automotive:** Engine ECU, ABS, ESP are all HIL-tested before a single prototype car is built.
- **Aerospace:** Fly-by-wire systems are validated in HIL rigs simulating 6-DOF flight dynamics.
- **Robotics:** Surgical robots like the Da Vinci undergo MIL ŌåÆ SIL ŌåÆ HIL ŌåÆ in-vitro ŌåÆ in-vivo progression.
- **Industrial Automation:** PLC-based assembly lines use real-time digital twins for predictive maintenance testing.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation: Discretising a PID Controller

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context (Typical KTU style):** Given a continuous PID controller
> $$C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$$
> derive the **velocity-form digital PID** suitable for microcontroller implementation, starting from the continuous-time derivative.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Write the continuous-time control law in time domain

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Approximate the integral with the rectangular (forward) rule

$$\int_0^{kT_s} e(\tau)\,d\tau \;\approx\; T_s \sum_{j=0}^{k} e[j]$$

This is the **trapezoidal accumulation**: each sample contributes a rectangle of height $e[k]$ and width $T_s$.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Approximate the derivative with the backward-difference formula

$$\frac{de(t)}{dt}\bigg|_{t=kT_s} \;\approx\; \frac{e[k] - e[k-1]}{T_s}$$

The **Why**: The backward difference uses *known past* values only, making it **causal** and thus physically implementable on hardware that cannot see the future.

### Step 4 ŌĆö Form the position-form digital PID

$$u[k] = K_p \, e[k] + K_i T_s \sum_{j=0}^{k} e[j] + K_d \,\frac{e[k] - e[k-1]}{T_s}$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Derive the velocity (incremental) form to avoid integrator windup

Compute $u[k] - u[k-1]$:

$$\begin{aligned}
u[k] - u[k-1] &=
K_p (e[k] - e[k-1])
+ K_i T_s \, e[k] \\
&\quad + K_d \frac{(e[k] - e[k-1]) - (e[k-1] - e[k-2])}{T_s}
\end{aligned}$$

Simplify the derivative term:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta u[k] &=
K_p (e[k] - e[k-1])
+ K_i T_s \, e[k] \\
&\quad + \frac{K_d}{T_s} \bigl(e[k] - 2e[k-1] + e[k-2]\bigr)
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why velocity form?** If the actuator saturates at some sample, you can **freeze the accumulator** or **back-calculate** the integral ŌĆö no windup. This is the form most KTU 2024 examiner answers expect.

### Step 6 ŌĆö Sample-rate guideline (Shannon-Nyquist with safety margin)

$$f_s \geq 10 \cdot f_{BW} \;\;\text{or}\;\; T_s \leq \frac{1}{10 f_{BW}}$$

The factor **10** (not 2!) is engineering practice to retain phase margin after ZOH delay.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation: Anti-Aliasing Filter Design

A plant has a maximum natural frequency $f_{max} = 200$ Hz. Choose a sampling rate and design a 1st-order Butterworth pre-filter.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Set sampling rate (engineering practice):**
$$f_s = 10 \times f_{max} = 2000 \text{ Hz} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; T_s = 0.5 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Set the filter cut-off (Nyquist margin):**
$$f_c = 0.4 \, f_s = 800 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Realise as 1st-order RC:**

$$\tau = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 800} \approx 1.989 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Choose $R$ and $C$ (practical values):**
Pick $C = 100$ nF. Then:
$$R = \frac{\tau}{C} = \frac{1.989 \times 10^{-4}}{100 \times 10^{-9}} = 1989 \;\Omega \approx 2 \text{ k}\Omega$$

> [!NOTE]
> The pre-filter is **analog**, built from op-amp + RC, placed **before** the ADC. Without it, out-of-band noise above $f_s/2$ folds back into the signal band as **aliasing distortion**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Discretisation Method

| Method | Stability Preservation | Accuracy | Use When |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Forward Euler** | Not always | Low | Simple, slow loops; educational |
| **Backward Euler** | Yes (stable plant stays stable) | Medium | Actuator / integral terms (anti-windup friendly) |
| **Tustin (Bilinear)** | Yes, with pre-warping | High | Most digital redesigns, audio, motion control |
| **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)** | Exact for sampled-data | Exact | Default in MATLAB `c2d()` for continuous plants |
| **Matched Pole-Zero (MPZ)** | Preserves DC gain | Medium | Filters with known structure |

---

## 3.4 HIL System Mathematical Basis

A HIL rig replaces the real plant with a **real-time mathematical model** running on a real-time simulator (e.g., dSPACE, National Instruments VeriStand, OPAL-RT). The **plant dynamics** are described by a state-space ODE:

$$\dot{x}_p(t) = A_p x_p(t) + B_p u_c(t)$$

$$y_p(t) = C_p x_p(t)$$

The simulator solves this ODE numerically every $\Delta t$ using a fixed-step solver (e.g., RK4) to produce $y_p$ that the **real controller** reads as if it were a physical sensor. The **How**:

- $\Delta t$ must be **smaller than the controller's sampling period** $T_s$ for fidelity.
- I/O latency through DAC/ADC must be **bounded and known** to the controller.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Mechatronic Implementation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    reqA[System Requirements] --> modelA[Mathematical Plant Model]
    modelA --> designA[Control Law Design s-domain]
    designA --> disA[Discretisation s to z]
    disA --> milA[MIL Simulation]
    milA --> silA[SIL Verification]
    silA --> pilA[PIL Timing Test]
    pilA --> hilA[HIL Validation]
    hilA --> deployA[Flashed Firmware on Hardware]
    deployA --> rwA[Real-World Experiments]
    rwA --> certA[Certification and Sign-off]
    certA --> fieldA[Field Deployment]
    fieldA --> maintA[Feedback to Requirements]
    maintA -.-> reqA
```

## 4.2 V-Model for Mechatronic System Development

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph descent[Design Phase]
        A1[System Requirements]
        A2[Subsystem Design]
        A3[Component Design]
        A4[Coding]
    end
    subgraph ascent[Test Phase]
        B1[Acceptance Testing]
        B2[Integration Testing]
        B3[Unit Testing]
        B4[Static Analysis]
    end
    A1 --- B1
    A2 --- B2
    A3 --- B3
    A4 --- B4
```

## 4.3 Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph realCtrl[Real Controller Hardware]
        sense[ADC Input Pins]
        proc[Microprocessor or DSP]
        pwmout[PWM and DAC Output]
    end
    subgraph simPlant[Real Time Simulator]
        plant[Plant Model ODE Solver]
        outbuf[Analog Output Buffer]
        inbuf[Analog Input Acquisition]
    end
    sense -->|Actual Sensor Signals| proc
    proc -->|Control Command| pwmout
    pwmout -->|DAC Output to Simulator| inbuf
    inbuf --> plant
    plant --> outbuf
    outbuf -->|Emulated Sensor Signal| sense
```

## 4.4 Discrete Control Loop Timing Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant T as Timer Interrupt
    participant S as Sensor ADC
    participant C as Control Algorithm
    participant A as Actuator PWM
    participant P as Physical Plant
    T->>S: Trigger Sample at kT_s
    S-->>C: Read y k
    C->>C: Compute u k from e k
    C-->>A: Apply u k via PWM
    A->>P: Drive Plant
    P-->>S: New physical state evolves
    Note over T,P: Cycle repeats every T_s
```

## 4.5 Testing Pyramid (Fidelity-Cost Trade-off)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    top[Real World Field Tests Highest Cost, Highest Fidelity]
    mid2[Hardware in the Loop HIL]
    mid1[Software in the Loop SIL]
    base[Model in the Loop MIL Lowest Cost, Lowest Risk]
    base --> mid1
    mid1 --> mid2
    mid2 --> top
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for Students:** When you see a closed loop in a HIL block diagram, the *direction of signal flow* is what matters. The simulator fools the controller into thinking it is connected to a real plant ŌĆö this is the essence of HIL.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) simulation. Mention two of its advantages over purely software-based testing.
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024 Model Paper, PERAT861, Module 4]**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **HIL Simulation** is a real-time testing technique in which a physical controller (e.g., ECU) is connected to a real-time simulator that mathematically emulates the plant's behaviour, exchanging realistic sensor and actuator signals.
>
> **Two advantages:**
> 1. **Realistic I/O behaviour** ŌĆö The controller sees actual voltage levels, noise, and timing of real ADC/DAC channels, which pure software SIL cannot replicate.
> 2. **Safe fault injection** ŌĆö Dangerous faults (sensor failures, shorts, over-voltages) can be injected into the simulated plant without risking real hardware or human life.
> 3. *(Bonus 3rd)*: Enables parallel testing of multiple controller revisions against the same plant model ŌĆö full repeatability.

---

### Question 2: Why is the **velocity form** of the digital PID preferred over the **position form** in practical microcontroller implementations?
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, PERAT861, Module 4]**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The velocity form computes $\Delta u[k] = u[k] - u[k-1]$ rather than $u[k]$ itself.
>
> **Two reasons:**
> 1. **Anti-windup friendly:** If the actuator saturates, the integrator does not accumulate unboundedly because the control *increment* $\Delta u$ can be clamped, and the controller can switch to **back-calculation** of the integrator state.
> 2. **Bumpless transfer:** During manual-to-automatic mode changes, the controller can resume from the last applied actuator value $u[k-1]$ without sudden jumps.
> 3. *(Bonus)*: Only past errors $e[k-1], e[k-2]$ are needed ŌĆö no need to store the entire error history $\sum e[j]$, saving RAM.

---

## Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain with a block diagram the **complete pipeline** for implementing a continuous-time PID controller on a microcontroller. List the key steps and identify two issues that arise during hardware deployment. **(7 marks)**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022, PERAT861, Module 4 ŌĆö adapted]**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Pipeline steps (4 marks):**
> 1. **Plant modelling** ŌĆö Derive $G(s)$ of the physical system experimentally (step response or frequency response) or analytically from first principles.
> 2. **Controller design** ŌĆö Tune $K_p, K_i, K_d$ in continuous time using Ziegler-Nichols, root locus, or pole-placement.
> 3. **Discretisation** ŌĆö Convert $C(s)$ to $C(z)$ using Tustin's method with pre-warping: $s \rightarrow \dfrac{2}{T_s}\dfrac{z-1}{z+1}$.
> 4. **Code generation** ŌĆö Either auto-generate C from Simulink Embedded Coder or hand-code the velocity-form PID equation in C.
> 5. **I/O configuration** ŌĆö Set up ADC channels for sensor inputs, PWM modules for actuator outputs, and encoder interfaces where applicable.
> 6. **Real-time scheduling** ŌĆö Bind the control task to a hardware timer interrupt with priority higher than any background task.
> 7. **Commissioning** ŌĆö Load the firmware, observe step response, re-tune gains manually or via auto-tuning.
>
> **Two hardware issues (3 marks):**
> 1. **Quantisation noise:** A 10-bit ADC with 3.3 V reference gives $\Delta = 3.3/1024 \approx 3.2$ mV step, which limits achievable steady-state accuracy.
> 2. **Computational / sampling delay:** If the loop period $T_s$ is too long relative to plant bandwidth, phase margin is eroded. The Zero-Order-Hold contributes $\phi_{ZOH} \approx -\omega T_s/2$ extra lag.
> 3. *(Optional 3rd)*: Integrator windup when actuator saturates.
>
> **Block diagram reference:** See SECTION 4.1 of these notes.

---

**(b)** Compare **MIL, SIL, and HIL** testing methodologies in terms of fidelity, cost, risk, and typical stage of use. Give one engineering example where HIL is indispensable. **(7 marks)**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024 Model Paper, PERAT861, Module 4]**

> **Model Answer (tabular form expected by KTU valuator):**
>
> | Aspect | MIL | SIL | HIL |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **Plant** | Simulated (math) | Simulated (math) | Simulated on real-time HW |
> | **Controller** | Simulated (math) | Compiled code on PC | Real hardware / ECU |
> | **Fidelity** | Lowest | LowŌĆōMedium | High (real I/O timing) |
> | **Cost** | Lowest | Low | High (real-time simulator) |
> | **Risk** | Lowest | Low | Medium (controller can be damaged) |
> | **Stage of use** | Early algorithm design | Post-coding verification | Pre-deployment integration |
>
> **Example where HIL is indispensable (3 marks):**
> **Automotive Anti-lock Braking System (ABS) ECU.** The ABS controller must react within **5ŌĆō10 ms** to wheel-speed sensor data. Pure software simulation cannot validate the exact interrupt latency, ADC conversion time, and PWM update behaviour of the production ECU. HIL lets engineers simulate a four-wheel vehicle model in real time, inject sensor faults (broken wire, short-circuit), and verify that the production ECU handles them safely ŌĆö without ever slamming a real car into a wall.
>
> **Other valid examples:** Aircraft fly-by-wire systems, surgical robots, nuclear reactor control rods.

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö INTERNAL CHOICE

**(a)** Derive the **velocity-form digital PID controller** starting from the continuous-time PID equation. Explain why Tustin's method is preferred over Forward Euler for discretising a derivative term in a control loop. **(7 marks)**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, PERAT861, Module 4]**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Derivation (5 marks):** See full derivation in **SECTION 3.1** of these notes. Key milestones the examiner expects:
> 1. Start with $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e\,d\tau + K_d \dot e(t)$.
> 2. Apply rectangular integration: $\int_0^{kT_s} e\,d\tau \approx T_s \sum e[j]$.
> 3. Apply backward difference: $\dot e(kT_s) \approx \dfrac{e[k]-e[k-1]}{T_s}$.
> 4. Write $u[k]$ (position form).
> 5. Compute $\Delta u[k] = u[k] - u[k-1]$ to obtain the velocity form.
>
> **Why Tustin > Forward Euler for derivative terms (2 marks):**
> - Forward Euler maps the $j\omega$-axis to a circle **inside** the unit circle in the $z$-plane, distorting the frequency response and **shrinking** the Nyquist frequency ŌĆö this can destabilise marginally stable systems.
> - Tustin's method maps the $j\omega$-axis **onto** the unit circle (frequency-preserving), preserving stability margins and giving a more accurate digital approximation of the derivative action.
> - With **pre-warping** at the design frequency, Tustin matches the magnitude and phase exactly at that frequency.

**(b)** Describe the **V-Model** for mechatronic system development. For each design phase on the left leg, state the corresponding test on the right leg, and explain why this pairing is essential. **(7 marks)**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2023, PERAT861, Module 4]**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> The V-Model is a systems engineering framework where each development phase has a directly corresponding test phase (see diagram in **SECTION 4.2**).
>
> **The four pairs (4 marks):**
>
> | Left Leg (Design) | Right Leg (Test) |
> |---|---|
> | 1. System Requirements | 1. Acceptance Testing |
> | 2. Subsystem Architecture | 2. Integration Testing |
> | 3. Detailed Component Design | 3. Unit Testing |
> | 4. Implementation / Coding | 4. Static Analysis & Code Review |
>
> **Why pairing is essential (3 marks):**
> 1. **Traceability** ŌĆö Every requirement (left) is *objectively testable* through one acceptance test (right). This is mandatory for ISO 26262, IEC 61508, and DO-178C compliance.
> 2. **Early fault detection** ŌĆö If unit tests fail, the error is isolated to the *component design* level rather than discovered months later during field trials.
> 3. **Cost reduction** ŌĆö The earlier a defect is caught, the cheaper it is to fix. Industry studies show that a bug caught at the requirements stage costs 10ŌĆō100├Ś less than the same bug caught in the field.
> 4. **Risk mitigation** ŌĆö Especially for safety-critical mechatronic systems (surgical robots, autonomous vehicles), the V-Model provides documentary evidence that *each* function was verified.

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Module 4 Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Verification with Validation** ŌĆö KTU examiners regularly deduct 1ŌĆō2 marks when students use these interchangeably. *Verification = "Are we building it right?"*; *Validation = "Are we building the right thing?"*
> 2. **Forgetting anti-aliasing filter** ŌĆö When asked about sampling, students often write $f_s > 2 f_{max}$ but forget that **a real analog low-pass filter must precede the ADC**. KTU explicitly tests this.
> 3. **Mixing position and velocity PID** ŌĆö The two forms are NOT mathematically identical in the presence of actuator saturation. Be explicit about which form you are deriving.
> 4. **Ignoring ZOH delay** ŌĆö Many students write the closed-loop transfer function as $C(z)G(z)$ and forget the Zero-Order-Hold block, which contributes a half-sample delay that erodes phase margin.
> 5. **Calling MIL "HIL"** ŌĆö HIL involves *real hardware* of the controller; MIL does not. Examiners use this distinction as a discriminator between A-grade and B-grade answers.
> 6. **Not citing a sampling rate** ŌĆö When designing any digital control system, the answer must quote $T_s$ or $f_s$ explicitly. Skipping this loses 1 mark.

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Module 4**

- **Implementation Pipeline:** Plant model ŌåÆ Continuous controller ŌåÆ Discretise ŌåÆ Code ŌåÆ Cross-compile ŌåÆ Flash ŌåÆ Commission.
- **Sampling Rule (Engineering):** $f_s \geq 10 f_{BW}$, *not* just $2 f_{BW}$.
- **Discretisation Choices:** Forward Euler (simple, unstable-prone), Backward Euler (integrator-friendly), Tustin (best for derivative terms, use with pre-warping).
- **Anti-Aliasing:** Analog LPF before ADC with $f_c \approx 0.4 f_s$.
- **ADC Quantisation:** $\Delta = V_{ref}/2^n$; SNR = $6.02n + 1.76$ dB.
- **Velocity-Form PID:** $\Delta u[k]$ form; anti-windup friendly, bumpless transfer, low RAM.
- **ZOH Delay:** Adds $\phi_{ZOH} \approx -\omega T_s/2$ ŌĆö a non-negotiable penalty of sampled-data control.
- **Testing Hierarchy (low ŌåÆ high fidelity):** MIL ŌåÆ SIL ŌåÆ PIL ŌåÆ HIL ŌåÆ Real-world.
- **Verification vs Validation:** "Right" vs "Right system."
- **V-Model:** Requirements Ōåö Acceptance, Subsystem Ōåö Integration, Component Ōåö Unit, Code Ōåö Static Analysis.
- **HIL Use Case:** Safety-critical ECUs (ABS, fly-by-wire, surgical robots) where real faults must be injected without real-world harm.
- **Hardware Platforms:**
  - MCU ŌĆö cheap, general-purpose
  - DSP ŌĆö fast math, motor control
  - FPGA ŌĆö parallel, deterministic
  - PLC ŌĆö industrial, slow scan
  - Industrial PC + RTOS ŌĆö high-level algorithms
- **Jitter > Latency:** Determinism matters more than raw speed for control loops.
- **Validation Documentation:** Always pair each design step with a corresponding test step (V-Model compliance).

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Discretisation Choice:** **"T-B-F"** ŌĆö **T**ustin for derivative terms, **B**ackward Euler for integrators, **F**orward Euler for teaching only.
